굳이 긴 시간내고 물가대비 비싼 입장료 내고 갈 필요없음 가는 길 내내 내리막길에 흙탕물이 내려가서 다들 미끄러질뻔하고 볼 것도 없음 모든 상점이 똑같은 공산품만 팔고 있움
Eintritt gezahlt, gefühlt für Nichts. Künstliches Dorf. Nichts Authentisches.
입구부터 계속 가게만 있어서 입장료가 아깝네요.
Un lieu touristique .. rien à faire, à part prendre des photos dans un endroit fake 😅 …
베트남 치고 물가가 너무 올랐고 비쌉니다. 바가지도 심해요.
Nur für Touristen. Ich würde nicht mal kostenlos wiederkommen.
Turistada, super artificial todo!
Zu teuer 150.000 VND. Was für Chinesen. Gleicht einem Zoo.
소수민족 전통마을이라했는데 그냥 유원지임
상업주의 비오면 가지마세요 시간낭비. 그리고 제발 운동화 말고 슬리퍼같은거 신고가세요
Attrape touriste. Passez boire un café c'est suffisant
Это не стоит потраченных денег. Рекомендую пройти мимо.
N’y aller surtout pas !!
Aucun intérêt, complètement artificiel
רחוב חנויות מיותר בזבוז כסף מוחלט
Piège à touristes Entrée : 150 000 vnd
비싼 입장료내고 장사꾼들 얼굴 구경하고..후회막급!
Esta armado y el personal es agresivo si siguen un poco más encuentran pueblos más originales
Très cher
ไม่สนใจลูกค้าเลย no goods
Скучно, смотреть нечего, только фоткаться
너무 인공적이고 볼게 없음
절대 네버 절대 네버
C'est un attrape touristes
It was pretty much everything we tried to avoid while in Sapa. Shopping shopping all the same stuff and being harassed every step of the way. Skip it... not worth it.
Too commercialised. Don't bother to use the transport from the cultural village exit because it's VND10K for a 2min ride.Wouldn't recommend.
Except for the beautiful cool weather. And for picture taking enthusiasts, this man made village was built just to attract tourists.... nothing much to see....

Cat Cat Village is a beautiful place with amazing scenery and unique local culture. The views are truly worth visiting and walking around the village can be a great experience.However, one thing really spoiled our visit — some locals approached us offering traditional costume rental and photography services. At first it sounded nice, but they kept pushing us to hire their photographer. They demanded money on the spot and promised to edit the photos beautifully. Unfortunately, this was a scam. The photos we received were of very poor quality, not edited at all, and nothing like what they had promised.It’s sad because the village itself is wonderful, but these dishonest practices make the experience less enjoyable. Please be very careful and avoid these services so you can truly enjoy the beauty of Cat Cat Village without being scammed.

Contrary to the name, not a single shadow of cats. Tourist resort with some glimpses for photos to be published on social media. You pay about 5 euros the entrance.At the beginning there are stalls displaying traditional clothes to rent to tourists to tour the village like locals. Stalls everywhere.We were there in the middle of the week and early in the morning around 9.30 so it was not crowded.The view of the falls is nice.There is a 3 km walk (flat/downhill) to do to get there and, when you exit, to reach the bus yard are another 400 MT on foot. We took the shuttle for 10000 dong per person (about 1 euro at the current exchange rate). Is it worth going?A visit, yes.But without buying souvenirs or spending too much time on them.
Not worth going and waste of money. It does not have the traditional people living there. all commerical now.Visited 10 years ago was great but not now.

Admission for a fee. No interest, no authenticity.It's like an amusement park with shops every 5 meters. You're wasting time and money.You will only see tourists dressed as Hmong. The only advantage is that it makes you walk well.

Firstly, this is NOT an original village.  It is a reenactment. EVERYTHING is artificial, well, the plants and animals are real, but you get the drift.  Including the young children you think are in class. They are all hired acts.  It's just a nice place to take picturesque photos (if that is your cup of tea) and have easy access to a lot of local souvenirs.  There is also a cultural show that runs quite frequently.  Coffee is mediocre and over priced, but it's a 100% tourist stop, so that is to be expected.  The walk up and down is rather steep, so wear suitable shoes.

Basically…it’s a tourist trap. It’s not an authentic village, it’s new and built just for foreigners. You’re asked to buy something around every corner and even have to pay to stand on some terraces. Besides all that … it’s still a photogenic place and rather stunning to walk around. So catch 22

Ancient and quaint village but the definition of a place ruined by tourism and touts. Relentless steps and pestering at almost all of them. The circular hike is just a steep and busy main road. Bright sides are the people are friendly and they do have some nice souvenirs. I think a moment that sums up the experience was a lady who didn't bother to look up and just said "Hello, buy something."

I would not recommend going to Cat Cat village as it is just one big souvenir shop. Hardly any signs of what the traditional village was like snd the waterfall at the bottom is nothing special. Go to one of the other outer villages if you want to see something more authentic.
It's usually less interesting, nothing special. Just a small area around the river with waterfall views.

Nothing is authentic, everything is commercialized. If I would have realized that, I would not have gone. Yes, it is pretty but other places in Sapa are much prettier. If you don’t plan on renting the dresses, having make up done, and getting photos taken- I don’t recommend it.

This wasn’t worth the visit for us. You first have to walk through about a mile (for real) of small shops with pushy sellers. It is a long down hill walk with steps and steps in areas. Finally at the bottom the place is picturesque, but not much to do once you are there. Overall we wish we did something different with our time, this felt like a typical tourist trap.

Terribly touristy. Nothing authentic. There are much nicer areas and nicer villages. You pay to get in and then get hassled by all the people who want you to buy some of them. Also at the waterfall it is all commerce

The biggest tourist trap ever!You pay entrance to walk past a row of souvenir shops.Nothing authentic, nothing charming, just a flop!And then you also come out at a different point than where you enter which makes returning to Sapa longer.Was definitely not worth the time, effort and money!

Not a really interesting place to visit unless selfies are your thing. Loads of shops selling the same stuff and people blocking paths to take more photos. Because the road is blocked there are many motorbikes waiting at the top and bottom and driving up and down the road spruiking for business. Not enjoyable at all.

Unless you are interested in shopping or taking pictures in traditional clothing, its not really worth the entrance fee (150k p.P.). The village could be nice, but seems to suffer from overtourism. It was overcrowded (sunday) and you couldn't really enjoy it due to the amount of people, who all blocked paths to take pictures of themselves. It's just a big tourist town with people putting on a show to try and entertain. Left me feeling a bit sentimental about what this village may have been before.
The place itself is nice, but too commercial. It wasn't clear where the fake part ended and when the real village began. Disneyland in Sapa. Sin. Zero authenticity.
Tourist trap, worst experience in Vietnam so far. Read the other reviews, I agree.

If you like a walk through markets with people that go on at you to buy stuff, Chinese tourists who like dressing up in local clothes having pictures taken, litter, noise and far from authentic village then you must go! Absolutely horrendous! I had a few moments away from the chaos to appreciate the beauty surrounding the madness.

With an entry fee to the village of 150k dong per person, you expect to be immediately immersed in local architecture and culture. What you get however, is a series of distasteful highly commercial shops selling generic factory made stuff that you could get at any other cheap market in the region. This is while the sellers badger you aggressively. This covers 70% of the village walk. This is followed by a quite picturesque area overshadowed by tacky restaurants which incidentally gave both of us food poisoning. The traditional dance show performers were visibly miserable - in stark contrast with the performers in Fansipan who genuinely seemed to enjoy themselves and transferred this energy to the crowd. In among the commercial chaos one can find some truly handmade pieces but these are far and few between. The whole thing felt like they were just trying to squeeze money out of you without giving you the experience that you paid for. Even at the end where there were electric golf carts to take visitors back to the entrance, you had to pay for a 400m drive. If you go, know what to expect, don't let anyone oversell it to you and perhaps consider bringing your own food.

Visiting in wet December and if you were a Gen X and if you liked the risks of slipping down stairs and if you liked crowds and mind numbing shops selling same old “sane” old , then try this manufactured village
It is all very staged, no authentic feels. Not for me. Wish I had done some research earlier. To me, it was a waste of time.
It has become an “amusement park” — far too commercialized.  This was not the peaceful beautiful area we expected.  I visited this area in 2009, and since then, it has become far too developed.

Entrance fee is 150k per person, motorbike in is 50k per bike. (You can play jenga and see how many people can sit on 1 bike)Take the bike in, walk out.The nicest part of this village is during the walk out, you can see views of the paddy fields, explore an antique rose garden and a gorgeous (free) lookout point, look at huge pumpkins and gourds along the way. I’m still not sure it’s worth the money, it’s tiresome to have dozens of people asking if you want to take a bike. It’s reasonably pretty but my personal rating is about 6/10.

Most of the things are artificial. Unless you are an instagramer, you probably enjoy a small waterfall visit than this. First you have to pay for the entrance fee, second the route will lead you through countless streams of shops which sell goods overprice, third every toilet will cost you money and fourth you will experience a large crowd who are dressed up just for photographs.

The place hardly seems like a real village.Too commercialised and very crowded. All shops on both sides and vendors prompting you all the time.A trip would  consist of considerable steps going down to the waterfall and then back again. On the whole, I felt it was not worth the time and effort since we were in Sapa for just 2 days. When we visited the Ta van village next day, it felt a quaint little paradise compared to Cat Cat.If you are looking for a serene and quaint experience and a peep into the real village life and if you are short of time, I think Cat Cat should be avoided. Visit Ta Van or any other similar village.

Sehr auf Tourismus ausgelegt. Ist eher etwas für die asiatischen Besucher. Man kann sich Kostüme/Trachten ausleihen und stundenlang vor romantischen Kulissen fotografieren lassen. Uns haben die "echten, wahren" Dörfer wesentlich besser gefallen.

We decided to visit the village as it is a short distance from our hotel. This place is such a tourist trap, and is way too commercial and not for us. We were plagued by people constantly trying to sell us things, and at almost every shop we got, “hello, shopping, buy something” and we encountered some shop sellers and people trying to sell us motorbike rides who were pushy and unrelenting and who didn’t seem to take no for an answer with one person following us. It was all about hard sales and commerciality, and no information about the history of the village and how people live and it lacked authenticity. The positive side of our visit was that you do get good views of the rice fields and the agricultural practises that go on here. With the benefit of hindsight I wouldn’t have bothered. I felt bothered and harassed by the end of my visit and couldn’t wait to leave
It is very commercial. I'm willing to support cultural heritage, but tgis is a bit too much, despite the area is very nice.

Visited this "village" in April. Long walk down to the valley, passing along many shops selling stuff. At least the vendors weren't aggressively out hawking wares like some places in China but were quite gracious in allowing tourists to browse.Visited a couple of "homes", to see them weaving cloths, and to see the interior of a home. Felt like I was violating some personal space there!The payoff at the "village" is rather poor - perhaps this is the dry season, but the river is dry, the waterfall is small-ish, and the trees/plants are a bit dry and stunted. There were some horses, and structures for "Instagram" photos, a few animals but it took me a bit to realize that I was in the center of the "village". The touted hydro electric building had only a few pipes that were almost buried under the stage. (Did I miss seeing it or was this actually it?)The natural scenery as all over Sapa is beautiful, but the human (tourist agency?) intervention seemed out of place, intrusive at best, exploitative at worst.I understand the need for development, and tourism does improve livelihood of the community, but wither the balance between preserving-enriching culture (and exchange of) and exploiting the villagers (and tourists) for tourism sake?This and Sa Pa in general is a beautiful area, but it seems to be developing at a fast pace into a concrete, Instagram caricature of what is... kind of sad.

At this point, Cat Cat Village is more of a shopping complex than an authentic traditional experience. It's full of souvenir shops and costume. You can see that it's packed with local tourists wanting an artificial photo shoot opportunity. Foreigners, if you want a true village experience, trek to Lao Cai Village with a local guide instead.
Nothing authentic,just thousands of souvenir shops,probably everything made elsewhere...Too busy and one way so really a tourist "trap"

100,000 VND admission fee so you can walk for hours through souvenir stands - all selling exactly the same junk and dried nuts.  I’m not even certain the stuff is locally made.  No opportunity to leave the tour either - you’ll know you’re at the end when the drivers start harassing you to ride their motorbike.  The river is beautiful, but is ruined by attempts to make it more touristy - really tacky photo backdrops and fake flowers and whatnot.  Not worth the time or effort.

This place is very large, you can walk down to see things around but actually it’s not natural at all because people decored many things for tourists take photos..If wanna see whole place, you should come in day time, should not come after 4pm because you will not have enough time to walk up.

Cat Cat Village is a collection of toll entrances, tourist dump shops, dirt, dust, and more dirt. It is as authentic an ethnic village as Disney’s Epcot center, and has none of the charms of actual working Hmong villages with their gorgeous rice terraces that are scattered further out from Sapa center. Its only redeeming value is its proximity to Sapa itself, and even that is not enough to warrant a visit. Skip a visit to this “village” and you will not have missed much. The only enjoyment I derived from my visit was leaving it. Needless to say, I am not a fan.

The walk you are walking on is very hard to walk on.  It is rocks and stones sunk in dirt and mud and very easy to trip and fall if yo are not careful. I spent all of my time trying not to fall that I could not look at much of anything but where I was stepping.  Very hilly and lots of steps up and down.  Would not recommend for someone who cannot walk for a long time.  Took over two hours.  No turning back.

Sorry... maybe I've been spoiled on my travels... but we didn't enjoy this at all.Just felt so 'unreal' cheap and tacky.We walked down, following the road and then paid a small fee to enter the area.There were numerous stalls selling cheap souvenirs and also costume shops where people were hiring traditional costumes to dress up in and wander around the village, so there were many vietnamese dressed up all taking photo's of themselves.The waterfall at the bottom was lovely but that's about it.We couldn't see anyone really doing the handicrafts that were mentioned.We opted for a motorbike ride back up the steep hills to Sapa.It is certainly contradictory to the advertising.Personally I'd find somewhere else to visit.

The hydrology is of interest because the waterfalls come into the village from 3 different directions. Unfortunately, the village itself is kind of like Heritage Park meets Disneyland. There are functionless waterwheels, empty dovecotes, fake blossoms etc. It was full of young couples taking selfies. The craft demonstrations could have been interesting but there weren't any going on while we were there.

We did not enjoy our "trek" to CatCat Village.  We walked for about 20 minutes on the side of a very congested road with motorbikes and cars passing inches from us to arrive at CatCat Village.  I still cannot believe that was the "trek" part.  There is a small admission fee to enter the village.  At the village there are many many stalls selling souvenirs and lots of cheap stuff from China.  It was packed with Vietnamese tourists who rented costumes to wear and took photos of themselves at all the "selfie spots". Eventually we did see a pretty water fall and some people demonstrating local crafts, but it felt very artificial and touristy.  Not at all what we had imagined when we heard about trekking to a local village to see the lifestyle of the tribal people of the hill country.  I agree with jasperv772, it is ok, but there are real treks to small villages that are amazing.
無秩序な開発が横行し、ひどい環境です。民間業者が少数民族の村に適当な観光地をこしらえていてガイドに聞いても勝手に外から外来植物を持ってきて植えたものだとか、観光の現状を聞きました。
It's ok but not the reason you are coming to Sa Pa. View and town is nice, but also quite touristic. I would only recommend to do this if you have a few spare hours.

There are couple of nice spots here, and probably the nicest is to walk up the river that runs towards the lowest waterfall - it’s a bit out of the area and reqires some careful steps in the beginning, so it’s peaceful and you can get a little taste of jungle.The village itself is not nearly as ”original” or ”tribal” as advertised. Most waterfalls are artificial. Lobg walk to the village is stuffed with booths selling expensive souvenirs. Even though it’s mostly nice area, it’s a tourist trap, like whole of Sa Pa.

Een namaakdorp met veel winkeltjes en veel mogelijkheden voor selfies. Je kan er een traditionele outfit huren en poseren naar hartelust. 50m buiten dit dorp sta je in het echte leven maar er loopt geen mens. Kan me niet voorstellen dat je hiervoor naar Sapa komt ...
This village is cute but not authentic Hmong village. Shops and themed photoshoot set up. Need to go further into mountain villages to experience the true beauty of Sapa trip and the small villages.

Bản Cát Cát đã bê tông hoá rất nhiều nhằm phục vụ du lịch. Những nét hoang sơ như trong tranh ảnh không còn nữa mà thay vào đó là các tiểu cảnh nhân tạo để mọi người chụp hìnhĐặc biệt là nạn chèo kéo và chặt chém khách du lịch diễn ra thường xuyên rất khó chịu
Nie ma to nic wspólnego z wioska. Przechodzi się przez uliczki między sklepami i punktami gastronomicznymi i jeszcze trzeba za to zapłacić. Bardzo dużo ludzi.

Cat cat è solo una trappola per turisti, non c’è niente di autentico se non orde di cinesi agghindate con i costumi tipici del luogo nel tentativo di riuscire lo scatto perfetto per Instangram.Le guide non sono per niente indispensabili, con una buona applicazione sul telefono si può ammirare paesaggi fantastici,meno frequentati e villaggi più autentici!In definitiva, consiglio vivamente i trekking nelle risiere, ma cat cat è da evitare se si preferisce un po’ di autenticità!
Over 500m of shops with everything you can buy also in the City Centre. The prices in the village are to high. Example? Black coffee 50kVND and in the Town 25kVND.

I recommend to skip that village. It's like Disneyland and full of people who dress up in traditional clothes in order to make pictures literally everywhere. Wherever you turn, you bump into someone taking pictures or having their picture taken and you are always in the way. The waterfall is not worth the walk down. The stalls along the street all sell the same stuff. Avoid unless you enjoy kitsch loving crowds.

We travelled to this region because of the beautiful landscape and to learn about the H’mong ethnic groups that reside here. We have heard mixed reviews about Cat Cat Village from friends who visited it in previous years but we had a free afternoon so we took a walk believing we would be walking through a local village.  Instead we found ourselves at what was labeled a ticketing office.  It wasn’t exactly clear that this was a ticketing office for Cat Cat Village.At this point we googled to confirm that entry into what we believed was a village of indigenous people, admission would grant us entry into these people’s homes and a glimpse at culturally significant materials and the admission would go towards the local families.We walked down a steep set of steps lined with vendors in their small stalls, passed an endless stream of tourists dressed in rental traditional garb taking endless number of social media worthy photos, walked through well groomed and fenced flower fields and walked on questionable wood / bamboo walkways and stairs.  The only sign of local life were the children walking past us with bags of candy and farm homes off in the distance.  The only remaining sign of cultural relevance was the water wheels which no longer operate and if I hadn’t googled it, I wouldn’t have known they were used as power to pound rice.  And outside of the traditional dance show, I wouldn’t have known this was anything but a pretty, well groomed landscape.  As we neared what seemed to be the end of the attraction, I had to stop and compare what I was seeing to my sister’s photos from a year ago and it is very obvious that everything I saw was built in the last year. Comically enough, as we were enjoying a beer at one of the last stalls before the exit, we heard a bulldozer at the top of a hill and crashing sounds at the bottom. This bulldozer was pushing boulders down the hill without much care and every other boulder was smashing the walkway beneath it.  10 minutes passed before we saw a staff member came and put up a newly drawn “closed” sign leading up to that walkway. Thankfully it was nearing the end of the day and no one was walking in the direction of the former walkway.Much like the rest of Sa Pa, the boom of tourism seems to have repurposed Cat Cat Village from sharing the culture of the indigenous people to drawing tourists to an Instagram-worthy photo op.

People told us that it’s very close to Sapa and we can walk by ourselves, so we start walking and its a very dangerous thing to do, the locals driving like crazy, No sidewalks , and after almost two hours we arrived to the village, payed the entrance fees, we wasn’t Impressed at all, really cannot see something special about this place and really cannot understand what all the fuss was about and why people are recommending this place

If you're for looking an authentic village in sapa I wouldn't go here. It actually reminded me a bit of Disney land. If you do just wanna kill some time and go for a walk it wouldn't be the worst thing to do. You don't need a guide for this one. Just buy a ticket and walk down. Super simple.If you do go on a tour it is worth noting that they walk you all the way down to the bottom of the valley, and then attempt to hand you over to a pack of taxis for an additional cost to get back to the top. You can walk but it is a steep road with plenty of cars and scooters wizzing by.

Cat Cat, en la ladera de la montaña, es un parque temático, lleno de tiendas,  a ambos lados del camino, que conduce a unos saltos de agua. Lo más destacable quizá es ver fotografiarse a la gente de la zona con los trajes de fiesta. Prescindible.

Sitio totalmente orientado al turismo. Mi pareja y yo elegimos hacer este trekking pequeño por nuestra cuenta y la primera decepción fue que para entrar a hacer la ruta tuvimos que pagar 70.000 vnd por persona, admito que es poco pero para pasear no deberían cobrarte. Toda la ruta consiste en un camino lleno, a ambos lados, de tiendas y más tiendas con los mismos productos para turistas. Lo que es realmente triste es encontrarte, tanto en el Tour como en la ciudad de Sa Pa, la gran cantidad de niños explotados por sus mismos padres. Encontramos varias veces, sobretodo niñas, de no más de seis años cargando con bebés y intentándote vender pulseritas y llaveros, fue muy triste. Recomendamos que si podéis realicéis un tour por libre y uno que no sea este. La ciudad de Sa Pa ha perdido toda su originalidad parece una “mini” Benidorm.

If you want a more authentic experience, I'd look elsewhere. There is not much left of what I imagine the original village was like. Its now a bit of a gimmicky tourist trap - expect paved paths throughout, professionally constructed bridges and weirs, western music pumping from various  'shops' and paths lined with souvenir shop after souvenir shop. Its almost like a miniture theme park. Hilariously, groups of tourists and families were dressed in traditional village clothes which they had purchased while many of the villagers themselves were wearing what you'd consider to be western clothes. Even funnier were the tourists asking for pictures with the dressed up tourists, not realising the difference.I would recommend maybe visiting some other villages in the area which have not lost their authenticity. Unfortunately i didnt get a chance myself so cant make any recommendations.On a positive note, there are some nice spots for pictures. But I suspect you can get equally nice pics at other locations.
입장료내고 들어가는 구간을 한바퀴 다돌면 한시간 반정도 걸리는것 같아요이동하는 내내 어딜가나 있는 기념품과 제품을 팔고, 어린 아이들이 물건들고 다가오고..불편했습니다.특별한건 딱히 찾지 못했어요.그냥 입구에서 옷 빌려입고 기념촬영을 할수있다는정도?계곡까지 내려가고 한바퀴를 돌았다면 그냥 호객하는 오토바이 타고 올라오시는걸 추천드려요 걸어가려면 꽤 멀어요

We decide to spend our extra time in the morning to Cat Cat Village. There isn't much to see, lots of bamboo huts and shops, some water mills, flowers and a waterfall. The waterfall is the most impressive among its all. The place do not felt authentic and it seems that the Vietnamese came here only in the afternoon to do business. Would only recommend if you have extra time in Sapa.The village still have a long way from the ticket booth. Would recommend to sit a taxi from Sapa city directly to the village, because we walk a loooooong way to the place, it took us 30 minutes from Sapa city to the ticket booth , and another 30 minutes to the village itself.
Cảnh quan đẹp, có sự đầu tư. Tận dụng được địa hình cũng như vốn có của tự nhiên Sa Pa. Có ruộng có rừng có thác. Nhưng thái độ phục vụ của nhân viên quá tồi, đặc biệt là bạn bán vé tại cổng.

This village is quite beautiful, but does not feel authentic, it seems to have been changed into what is it today simply for all the tourists that visit. I also watched a dance there but it was not very good.

Total tourist trap, little kids begging on behalf of their parents. Its a pretty sad picture. You pay about 2-3 usd to enter so you can be hustled by taxi drivers and shops selling the same junk. Its sad to see the locals moving to tourism, getting lazy and treating everyone who visits as a mobile ATM instead of sticking to their culture and customs. AVOID!

Appeared to be a phony village designed to sell tourists many kinds of trinkets handmade by villagers. Waterfall at base of ‘village’ is nice but was so crowded when we were there we couldn’t get very close.
This place attract alot of tourist everyday. You have to walk down and up to pass thousand of stairs to see only TOURIST there

This is one of the most famous activity center of Sapa, but for some reason everything seems too artificial and sadly this was our one of the worst paid visit in Vietnam. We walked almost 7-8 km to see almost nothing. Waterfall was good, but beside that nothing!!

Nous sommes allés à pied de Sapa au village de Cat cat. L'entrée est payante comme tous les villages mais on a l'impression que c'est artificiel ou même de rentrer dans une sorte de réserve ou les habitants subissent notre passage. Ils sont tous là pour vendre leur artisanat et je ne me sentais pas à mon aise. On arrive ensuite à une cascade avec des artisans en démonstrations et un spectacle de danse. Je ne m'attendais pas à ça et quand j'ai visité les autres villages c'était très différent heureusement !

Too touristy to enjoy the village people living.  Everywhere were stall of so called villagers craft which look too commercialize.  I was told to bring some treats to the local children often deprived of simple indulgence like sweets and candy.To my sad finding, these little children has too many treats - sweets, lollipop, biscuits. A little will do good but too much will create dental problem and others problem to their little life.Spoke to the local and they agreed but there is nothing that they can do because they survive on tourist generated income. But it is unwise to give them money, they're not beggar. What he propose was if the village committee would set up an education fund for these children would be great. Then again, fund management would be a problem.Therefore the conclusion is to leave them alone to live as before. Visit only if you have no better place to go.

De stad kan de toeloop van toeristen niet aan. De vele gebouwen en hotels in constructie, gecombineerd met slecht onderhouden straten maken de stad onaangenaam. Wel goede startplaats voor meerdaagse trekking. Mooie natuur en vriendelijke mensen in de omgevende dorpen maken het wel de moeite waard.

We visited Cat Cat village because it was close and we only had a couple of days in Sapa.It is a giant tourist exploiting trap of no real value for the people whom it is meant to represent.There are women from the village on the roadside along the way and their job is to reel you in.It's awful, because as nice as it would be to spend some time talking to them and finding out about their lives, if you do engage, they just won't leave you alone and unfortunately it's all about getting you to spend money.There's an entrance fee (70 vnd pp)to walk through the village, we chose to do it ourselves rather than with a guide. It took about 2 hours there and back, it was pretty steep down hill then uphill on the return. On the way back,  motorbike touts offering rides back, very persistent. The stalls pretty much sold the same stuff, handwoven costumes, food ,one place sold essential oils and unfortunately there was $2 shop crap for sale too (why?).Perhaps the saddest thing I saw was small children no older than 4 or 5 carrying on their backs a little baby. The weight for their tiny backs was too much, never mind the obvious risk to both should they trip over and fall. There were staged scenes of small children sitting on swings - real kodak moments- at a price.The children ran around eating sweets, drinking coke and a few of them had rotten teeth. Pretty sad. I really felt that the most precious people in their community, their children ,were getting exploited and that is disgraceful anywhere in the world.This place (like a lot of Vietnam) is litter city. People just drop their waste on the ground, consequently parts of Cat Cat village were pretty slummy.It was beautiful down at the bridge and waterfall, they had gone to a lot of trouble to make it look fresh, it showed. There were performers too, that was nice to see.

Although this is described as a village, this is actually a village that is owned by one person. All of the people you see in the village are actually workers and they do not live there. It’s like a Disneyland of northern Vietnam. There were so many people that it was difficult to walk. Small children are asking for money. Vendors selling things on both sides of you. The waterfall was beautiful but there are much better places to visit that this tourist attraction.

The place is specially curator for tourism to experience small cultural aspect. Well maintained and clean. But its not for visitor who dislike touristy mock up sets. If we had know we would have avoid this attractions.  But the walk from CatCat Village back to town was really pleasant and offers nice views of surrounding Sapa.

Not worth your time and money. Huge tourist trap,  you pay to walk around tacky stands that are trying to sell you their 'handmade' products. Nothing unique or authentic about it. Giving it 2/5 for a decent waterfall that's in the middle of the village.

Every organized tour will bring you here, so be prepared for the masses. I wouldn't consider it a nature trek, but a march through the local version of a strip mall. This is a small village - there is no way the locals can crank out that much inventory of hand made crafts. I suspect a lot of it was manufactured in China. There was a loom with 3 women hanging around it, but I never saw anyone working the loom. I suspect that was all for show to lend it some authenticity. I suggest if you desire real insight into local culture to research homestays away from the organized tours.

우리나라로 치면 민속촌 또는 프로방스 마을 같습니다. 마을 자체는 아기자기하게 꾸며져 있으나 몹시 인공적이고 초입부터 전부 상점입니다. 상점,슈퍼,레스토랑으로 줄지어져 있으며 민가는 볼 수 없습니다. 사진을 찍기에는 좋습니다. 만약 소수민족의 삶을 보고싶으신 거면 추천하지 않습니다. 입장료만 없었어도 그러려니 하는데 70,000동의 입장료를 내며 갈 필요가 있나 싶습니다. 사파에 왔는데 시간이 남는다면 들리기에는 괜찮으나 여기를 보러 굳이 찾아 올 필요는 없습니다. 그리고 물건은 무조건 깎으세요. 저는 180,000동 이라는 가방을 흥정 2번만에 50,000동에 사는 분들 봤습니다. 그리고 염색된 물건들은 후처리를 안해서 무조건 물이 빠지니깐 빨래시 꼭 주의하세요!!

Es un pueblo totalmente ficticio y montado para el turista, por lo que no tiene interés ninguno. La entrada vale 70mil y es una turista así que mejor ahórrate el dinero y camina por las montañas de la zona, así seguro verás más vida local que en este pueblo.Nos recordó a Hobbiton pero con los recursos y el estilo vietnamita.

I don’t know why they ask an entrance fee for this village. There is a not particularly spectacular waterfall and some decorative watermill wheels, but the village itself is certainly the least authentic village we have hiked through during our days in Sapa. However, it is slightly entertaining to observe rich Asian girls dressing in traditional clothes and posing for an Instagram photo shoot. Even more entertaining / concerning are the Western tourist groups, who think they are visiting a remote village community and have therefore brought a big bag of candy to hand it out to everyone who looks local (including our own hiking guide). However, everyone should know that this not a great idea as this contributes to oversupplying the local kids with sugary sweets and giving them an incentive to linger around the streets rather than being in school. This is a tourist hotspot with thousands of visitors a day, though not with much to see!

Teleurstellende ervaring. Het is een openluchtmuseum, op toerisme gefocussed en weinig authentiek. Als je een paar dorpjes doorloopt tref je dat nog wel, maar Cat Cat is een farce. Iedereen loopt je het geld uit de zakken te kijken. Erg vervelend.

Show village, like a little theme park for the multitude of tourists. Easy walk down the hill from Sapa, but really just the start of quite a beautiful trek if you continue on the road to Y Linh Ho and beyond. Was a bit of a relief to leave Cat Cat and it’s trinkets behind to be honest.

We arrived in Sapa one afternoon and began a trek on the next day. So we decided to go down Sapa in order to enjoy the few hours we had left.Well, I do not know where to begin…You are forced to pay an entrance fee, just to enter the Cat Cat zone (we tried to avoid it by making a detour, but impossible: they found a way to “block” all the paths and make you pay). The price was 70k VN, which is expensive for what you see.We were literally harassed by people trying to sell you stuff all the way down the waterfall. It totally spoiled our afternoon. We were already kind of “used” to that attitude from Vietnamese people when they see tourists, but on that point of view, Cat Cat was the worse.We also saw a lot of children and teenagers dressed up (for tourists, I supposed) with whom we could take pictures. Well, some people seemed happy about it, I was rather bored.The waterfall itself was…nice. That’s the only word I find to define it. We enjoyed seeing it, but that’s it. We did not find any other activity for that day, so it was no waste of time but for sure, I do not advise anyone to do that activity.

Already at the entrance of village, you'll start understanding this village is not very authentic. Along the street they all sell the same souvenirs. When you get to the center of the village, you find a watermill. Not a real one, because they have hadropower electricity for a long time. They put up these to make it more touristic.It's a great place if you like to take pictures from a manufactured village (looks nice on pictures). If you would like to experience how the locals live, you should definitely go to a different village.This is a tourist trap.

Cat cat village is the Disneyland of the Vietnamese hill villages this place is so "manufactured" it's a tourist trap. The village has some wonderful views and good photo opportunities. But it feels very fake with many shops and stalls selling the same old tourist crap. You must walk down a steep hill from Sapa town down many steps through the "souvenir" gauntlet to a waterfall which is quite nice once you arrive. If you're short on time cat cat village is alright I guess as it will only take a couple of hours to visit. But if you have time I would suggest going for a walk, there are many genuine villages in the area not to far away I would recommend.

Cat cat village couldn’t be depicted as a horrible experience as the walk is pleasant and the locals are nice, but it’s probably one of the most touristy village you can find in Vietnam. Nothing seems to be real anymore (even most of the waterfalls are artificial), the ethnicities are probably wearing their traditional costumes just for the tourists and what is sold in the stands hasn’t anything to do with their traditions (unless industrial ice creams and Coke light  has been part of their customs, but I doubt it). So, you may not completely hate it, but if you’re looking for authenticity, you don’t need to spent time -and money- in here.

No suelo opinar de atracciones pero esta me parece que lo merece. Estamos hablando de un paseo por un recorrido de tiendas y más tiendas en una sucesión de escaleras, donde no verás nada verdaderamente de interés porque incluso cuando te digan que alguien vive allí no será cierto. Una decepción total de toda la familia, y viniendo junto con adolescentes ya es raro coincidir. Nosotros incluso la hemos hecho con nuestra guía y más allá de ilustrarte algo del modo de vida de la gente hace unos años poco o nada de auténtico sabor local obtendrás.Aprovecho para recomendaros que si podéis ir a otro lugar menos turístico que Sapa para ver los arrozales y algún mercado local auténtico lo hagáis. El propio Sapa es un “truño” de lugar y seguramente lo hemos matado los turistas

An entrance fee of 70K is required to enter this full-on tourist market with scenic views. Small souvenir shops flood the entire walking range of the village. The view is great, but not as worth it as other villages around(eg. Shin Chai village). The whole trek takes about an hour or so, the only interesting part is the waterfall which is near the end of the trail. A lot of drivers will offer to drive you back to town for a very expensive price, so walk till the very end to take official taxis that has a fixed rate, which will be the cheapest. This place is definitely worth skipping if you’re interested in less touristy spots.
It's quite sad but this place is in no way authentic village. There is a lot of shops with suveniers and everything is done only for turists. In Sapa I would go somewhere else.

This is my 2nd visit after 4 years and i was disappointed as the place has changed for the worse. Feels like you're entering a tourist trap with shops aligned on both side of the pathway to the main attraction. River was dirty with a lot of rubbish.

I went to Cat Cat expecting a traditional experience but it purely a tourist trap. Shop after shop selling traditional goods for as far as the eye can see. Be careful at peak times as there's only one road in and one road out. It can get blocked very easily. Don't take a car or a motorbike, just walk from Sapa.

I imagine Cat Cat village was once upon a time a traditional village however it is now just a tourist trap and I honestly felt like I had paid to enter a market. The walk to Cat Cat village from Sapa is lovely - the views are awesome and there are some good photo opportunities. I'd recommend stopping by one of the little cafe/restaurants along the way to soak up the views! The entrance fee to Cat Cat is 70,000 VND and you are instantly met with shops on either side of the stairs selling exactly the same stuff. Other tourists dress up in the traditional Hmong dresses and pose for pictures. If you are unfortunate to go at a busy time then be prepared to be pushes and shoved by other tourists as your are walking up and down the stairs. If you love animals, you may find some things here quite distressing. The first upsetting  thing I saw were two domestic cats in a small cage. The second, a pig eating what I thought was just some normal pig food but upon a closer look, the pig was in fact crunching on the jaw bone and teeth of another animal!! We walked down to the "traditional market" towards the bottom of the village which was closed. But you could take photos with the Hmong children for a fee of course - something which I didn't quite like the idea of nor encouraging young children to beg for money / photos with tourists. I would honestly save yourself the 70,000VND and go to one of the cafe's along the route to Cat Cat and have a nice coffee or a few beers and enjoy the views!
Leuk bedacht om in het water diverse speeltuigen te situeren. Echter voor onze leeftijd is dat niet echt aantrekkelijk

Wir waren hier, weil wir nur noch einen halben Tag Zeit hatten. Dafür war es ok, aber weder der schwer zu findende Weg durch die Reisterassen war wirklich schön noch das Dorf. Das ist wirklich nur für Touristen und alles andere als authentisch. Ein Riesen Unterschied zu unserer Tour nach Thau Phin am anderen Tag. Man kann nur hoffen, dass von den 70k Dong Eintritt wirklich was bei den Einheimischen ankommt.
Unless you are on a hike that passes through Cat Cat, skip this tourist trap and go to more remote villages that are still unspoiled.

Vesnice Hmongů jsou krásné, původní, tato je výjimkou: plno turistů, vesnice má už do svého původního rázu daleko. Hodně klasických domů, které si mezi Hmongy staví Vietnamci.  Samé home stay, bar, stánky, nic pro nás.

We went here as it was close to Sapa town and we only had a day. Totally touristy and they were constructing 'old' buildings as we went through. Looks like a village that used to be picturesque so they pimped it out till it's all artifice now. May have been affected by the amount of tourists taking sa life's in every conceivable location

My title may be a bit harsh but it is what it is. Stall after stall of souvenirs and restaurants all the way down the stairs. Entrance fees are 70,000 Dong/pax ( buy at both directly across from gate). When you get to the bottom it’s quite pretty with the waterfall and quaint city. The village is worth a trip to if you have a free hour and don’t mind hordes of tourists. Go on a rainy day or very early or late to avoid the crowds.There are so many untouched places near Sapa that can be explored without the crowds and are worth more attention. It’s sad to see what tourism can create sometimes.

I must admit it right away - if you have just few hours of spare time or enjoy visiting touristy shops then I understand why you can come here and enjoy it. In any other case I would suggest to go elsewhere.The entire Cat Cat village is, IMHO, one big tourist trap. First you pay 70.000 VND per person for a chance to actually enter this place. The next thing you see are shops, shops, shops and restaurants, all labeled with signs like "authentic", "Hmong", "tribal", etc., you have to walk through to get to the waterfall. That one is OK, not worth 70.000 VND, IMHO, but OK. After that you can enjoy a walk over a small bamboo bridge, get chased by people trying to sell you stuff or just to get money and sweets.As you can see we didn't really enjoy it. Why? Well because there is so much more, better, more authentic and free (or at least cheaper) in the Sa Pa area than the Cat Cat village.Instead of this I would recommend to freely roam the rice fields and tribal villages down (= southwest) the valley from the Sa Pa town and the Cat Cat village. It will cost you 75.000 VND per person to get in but you can see so much more - all the beautiful rice fields, you can imagine, many animals, several waterfalls (all much better than the Cat Cat one), local villages, etc. In other words you can actually experience the are and see how people make their living.I believe there is no need to hire a guide as all the paths are pretty clear and you can see Sa Pa all the time thus you have a good reference point. Having a local guide is nice if you want to learn more about their lifestyle and the area itself, not to get around, IMHO.So to wrap it up - as stated above, if you have time and are willing to do some hiking, spend your time in the valley. If you are in a rush and want to see at least something, Cat Cat is alright, I guess.

Sa Pa is a must destination for most of the nort Vietnam visitors. The town is booming right now, many new hotels are being built. All those people that just spent one night in town are destined to visit this close by village. It is not all bad, but opt for couple of nights in a nicer villages further down the valley. Support local homestays and  get real experience in this awesome part of VietnamCat Cat village is just a first victim of mass tourism
This is a it like a Disney version of Sapa culture. We were disappointed by a long hike to and from this over crowded and over commercialised tourist attraction.

Catcat area, not worth the entrance fee If you’ve seen other places around sapa. 70k 1 person is pretty high for Vietnamese standards. We wanted to see the waterfall, but the walk to there wasnt that Good. One big stairway down, only tourist aimed selling houses. You cant find the authentic village anymore. The waterfall itself wasnt that great, all brown mud water. Pretty small stream. My conclusion: if its your First place to stop in sapa area and If you didnt see many waterfall before you Will probably like it. But If you Got spoiled like us I recommend to skip it.

Vorab : mittlerweile kostet der Eintritt 70000 Dong. Am Anfang war alles noch ganz nett aber nach und nach kam ich mir immer mehr wie in einem Freilichtmuseum vor. Ein Tourishop nach dem anderen läuft man bis zum Wasserfall. Es ist zwar alles ganz nett gemacht aber ich habe mir mehr versprochen. Ich würde zukünftigen Besuchern eher davon abraten und raten die Zeit besser zu nutzen.

The dirty town and super commercial.Not worth for the ticket price. Little fake for everything except waterfall. Waterfall is real but not beautiful. Even have good mountain view. But you can find the good view from another place.

Dies ist definitiv ein Dorf, welches nur für Touristen angelegt wurde. Man bezahlt 50'000 Dong um durch Strässchen voller Souvenirshops zu laufen. Der Wasserfall und die Wasserräder am Ende sind hübsch, mehr aber auch nicht.

El lugar en sí es bonito, pero está muy explotado por el turismo. Tienes que pagar para bajar por todo el pueblo, o mejor dicho tiendas, hasta llegar a la cascada. Cada vez que pasabas por una tienda alguien te dice el típico "Hello" para que compres, la verdad es que acabé cansada en ese aspecto. Luego los niños se me acercaban con el "Hello" y como pidiéndome algo que no entendía, hasta que vi a muchos turistas llevar bolsas llenas de chucherías y darles a los niños. No sé si esto era un protocolo o qué, pero la verdad es que salí muy decepcionada.

Laat me met de positieve zaken starten:-wandelafstand van Sapa (3,5 km).- Mooie omgeving, "dorp".Nu de minder fraaie kant van Cat Cat: Het is in scène gezet. Je betaalt inkom aan het begin van het dorp waarna je afdaalt in een soort "bokrijk" van Cat Cat. In de waterkracht centrale wordt afwisselend een film afgespeeld of traditionele dansen opgevoerd. De "dorpelingen" laten zien hoe men traditionele kledij maakt en verder is er nog de waterval.Wij werden begeleid door een gids die enigszins haast had en niets uitlegde dus mogelijks hebben we niet de volledige ervaring van Cat Cat gehad. Als men echt authentiek Vietnam wil zien kan men beter zelf de omliggende dorpen bezoeken.

Tourist trap. Need to be fit as stone stairs can be dangerous and slippery. Has lost all of its authenticity. Can be probably be described as a tribal theme park but the children are adorable. For authenticity you will have to further afield as cat cat is too close to sapa. Nice water falls at the bottom.

Un pueblo muy turístico.pagamos por entrar.te hacen pasar por todos sus puestos hasta llegar a lo bonito del pueblo.había una casa tradicional que podias entrar al verlo,pero la  señora que estaba dentro nos puso mala al hacerlo.cuando terminas de hacer el recorrido del pueblo te esperan unos cuantos taxis y motos para subirte al principio del pueblo.nosotros decidimos subir andando parando ya al final en un bar que nos encontramos.Nos ponen mala cara aquí también.No se,me parece bien que tengas que vivir del turismo,pero por lo menos tratarlo bien.
Demasiada gente y demasiadas tiendas. Hay que pagar una entrada para bajar al pueblo, no vale mucho, pero lo tienen tan enfocado al turismo que no tiene encanto. No lo recomiendo!

Au pied de Sapa, situé dans une très belle vallée, ce village n'est qu'une succession d'échoppes et n'offre pas beaucoup d'intérêt en dehors de quelques points de vue splendides ... D'autres ballades d'une dizaine de kms (ou plus) sont possibles et sont beaucoup plus intéressantes

There are many authentic villages of ethnic minorities in the area of Sapa... his isn't one of them. The "town" is nothing more than a shopping street with pushy old ladies and young children trying to sell local merchandise.
We were doing a trek by ourselves fron Ta Vanh to Sapa and it was compulsory to pass to arrive and also compulsory to pay 50.000 VNG. There are too many shops of souvenirs and no many things to do.

This is an easy walk from Sapa city. You dont need a guide, just follow the road. Its the only one there. You do have to buy an entry ticket, not to expensive.Then you can either follow the road (long way) or head straight left down the hill using some kind of stairs.From that moment its almost tourist shop after tourist shop. Thats not my kind of enjoyment. Then the waterfall is pretty nice, but i've been spoiled in Iceland. So not that big of a deal for me.
This " village" is mostly a fabrication to extract cash from tourists the local Hmong guides will telll you that the shopkeepers  are mostly not locals but Vietnamese

Cat cat being only 3km from Sapa, it is a easy walk downhill taking a hour. Upon entering, you are required to pay 50k dong, to enter the tourist trap. Very small village packed with hundreds of tourist taking selfies at several water wheels and waterfall. Very commercialized, and I much preferred the other villages like Ta Van and Lao Chain(far but an scenic trek).

Cat Cat Village is nothing more than a huge tourist trap with local village people selling the same thing. At the bottom of the steps through the village you are rewarded with a small filthy dirty waterfall. Do not waste your precious vacation time going to this village.
Hầu như không có gì để tìm hiểu, nghiên cứu về văn hóa dân tộc tại đây. Khu du lịch đã bị thương mại hóa quá nhiều.

the highlight of cat cat village is of course the waterfall and ancient hydro power station at the bottom: but to get there you have to go down a couple of miles of uncomfortable (at times slippery) stairs, through dozens of souvenir shops, and then walk all of that back up - also mostly by stairs. At least for people our age (late 50's, early 60's) this is not suitable. The waterfall is nice but not worth the pain in our knees and backs, and the so-called music and dance display they practically force you to watch once you've reached the bottom, on uncomfortable backless benches, is definitely not worth it.

This place was maybe once traditional, before all the turists started to come.Nowadays, everybody is selling some clothes and other things around the pathway, there is entrance of 50 000 to go to the village, full of annoying people taking pictures everywhere....you can get traditional wifi everywhere...the only interesting thing is quite nice waterfall and the bamboo bridge next to it...

We went back to Cat Cat village after our last visit 20 years ago. What a disappointment! This is now a 100% tourist attraction and you pay a ticket to basically access shops that are selling the same things than everything else. The site has been accommodate for tourist so you basically do a loop following everyone like a docile queue. The scenery is beautiful but frankly it is as nice all around Sapa by just doing a nature trekking. You will also learn more and have a much better discovery of minority's way of living by visiting "real" village around Sapa.

C'était le gros point noir de notre trek de 2 jours et malheureusement catcat village n'est pas le seul endroit,  ( voir photos) Beaucoup de touristes pensent bien faire en donnant des bonbons et gâteaux aux enfants. Du coup c'est halloween tous les jours dans les villages de sapa sauf que les enfants réclament, ne se privent pas et jettent les emballages partout. (face à des parents "impuissant")Donc svp, posez vous cette questions..!EST-CE QUE JE DONNERAIS AUTANT DE BONBONS A MES ENFANTS(sucre)????Candy = disease and pollution

We walked there from Sapa, it is not far, but quite steep on the way back. Compared to other villages it is very touristic and the main attractions are the shops on the way down. It is far better to do the trekking and visit some other villages.

We ended up walking through the village on the way to Tavan (I think we could have left Sapa another direction without the need to go through Cat Cat). It's basically a village built for tourists that you need to pay to enter. So if you're interested in a sanitized version of Hmong life as imagined for tourists who have no imagination...welcome.

If you’re interested in wearing traditional clothes or beautiful scenery, you’d love it. But if you want to learn their actual history or traditions, Google or Wikipedia can provide much better information than actually visiting here. Aside …

While Catcat Village is visually beautiful and the dress rental was fun, the treatment of the horse is disturbing. The horse looked overworked and clearly exhausted, even if only used for photo opportunities. Animal welfare should never be ignored for tourism.

Cat Cat Village has stunning views and is definitely worth visiting. Unfortunately, the experience was spoiled by some locals offering costume rental and photography services. They push you to hire their photographer, demand money on the …
Beautiful place which is an interesting walk but the only bit that’s actually nice is the end section where you are by the river and waterfall. …

Looks good in the photos, but completely fake and built for tourists. Some of the waterfalls are fed by piped water. If you like Disney land, this will be great for you. But if you want an authentic experience. Look elsewhere
A 'fake' village that was created as a tourist trap. I was amazed st the water falls until a tourist pointed out the man made pipes to create the fake waterfalls. …
This place felt overly touristy and lacked the charm of an authentic local village. It didn’t live up to expectations. Wouldn’t recommend.

Entry fee of 150k for a long alley of little shops, where every one of them offers you the same things as you can see in the city of Sapa. Would not recommend. Only the last part of the village near the waterfalls was a bit interesting.

We have booked grab to reach here.But till half road t grab stops and informed us that grab cannot go further & we must take motorbike around 150000vd. He t grab person should have inform before accepting booking or infor earlier! scamming …

150k/vé người lớn trẻ em 70k ( quá cao cho những gì nhận lại). Từ cổng soát vé đầu tiên đi bộ bậc thang xuống thung lũng mà 2 bên chỉ toàn các sạp bán đồ lưu niệm . Đến điểm trung tâm nhất thì toàn là những ngôi nhà mới xây dựng để kinh …

Cat Cat Village offers a glimpse into local culture, but our visit in May left much to be desired. The rainy and foggy weather made navigating the muddy paths a slippery challenge, so come prepared with slip-proof shoes. …

Cat Cat has long since lost it's small village charm. It's more of an outdoor market selling everything and anything you can get anywhere else in Sapa. Anything that was once to original village is hidden behind stalls and totally …
Cat cat village has an entrance fee of 150k per person. Some places in the village cost an additional fee (e.g., the tree house asks for 10k more per person). …

This is not a village, this is a market. This place is not even close to a traditional Vietnamese village. There are only a lot of shops with different stuff, some small waterfalls and some beautiful buildings. This is not worth 150 thousand.
(Xuyên Việt 2019) Đang hình dung một bản đồng bào đơn sơ, thiên nhiên, thân thiện để tụi nhỏ đi dạo quan sát đời sống, đặc sản... nhưng khá hụt hẩng, 1 khu du lịch "công nghiệp", …

I felt this place was definitely overrated. Before you enter the tourism village there is a steep decline to walk down, and the roads are lined up with shops all selling the same thing or renting the same constumes. After entering the …

The place is nice and perfect for tourist, because the place has been reworked, rebuild,  new decor, somehow lost its authenticity. I don't quite enjoy that, alot of shops selling souvenirs all the way. Good place to take pics and insta friendly.  The fees slightly expensive 150k dong. Just to view the souvenirs and waterfall.

Would recommend this place only if you really have no where else to go. It's really a touristy village that has been commercialised and an entrance fee is required as well. Also, certain photo locations within the village requires an …

Il n'y a plus rien de traditionnel la-dedans. Le site est joli, petites cascades, rivière, si vous montez les marches un peu partout vous avez des belles vues sur les montagnes tout autour, mais le village a été transformé en une vaste boutique. Quand il n'y a pas beaucoup de touristes, ça reste joli et même sympa, mais ne cherchez pas d'ethnologie ici-bas. 150 000 d l'entrée. Si vous venez à pied, la remontée est parfois raide. Ça reste très faisable. Il y a des cafés pour se reposer tout le long de la remontée vers la ville, avec une jolie vue (quand il fait beau).

Deze kan je gewoon overslaan. Na een lange wandeling moet je entree betalen. En waarvoor? Het dorp blijkt een gigantisch grote supermarkt te zijn vol met toeristische prullaria. Beter een stukje doorrijden en een wandeling maken door het dal om de prachtige landschappen te zien en vriendelijke mensen te ontmoeten zonder lastig gevallen te worden door prullaria verkopers.

Molto particolare,  ma ovvia “trappola per turisti”.  Purtroppo durante il ns soggiorno a Sapa la nebbia è durata tutta la giornata perciò abbiamo optato, su consiglio del ns hotel, per la visita al villaggio tipico di Cat Cat. Devo dire che è un posto suggestivo con ponticelli cascate ecc.. si paga un biglietto d’ingresso proprio all’entrata e poi si scende lungo il sentiero dove sono presenti negozietti  che vendono prodotti per turisti. Sempre lungo il sentiero trovate vari punti ristoro con pietanze tipiche e bagni (cosa molto importante). Sono presenti anche spiazzi e istallazioni per fare fotografie / selfie cosa che ha “rovinato” un po il clima reale del villaggio.  Non lo consiglio molto poiché a me personalmente ha lasciato una sensazione di finzione . Un vero peccato visto il territorio locale naturalistico veramente interessante.

Una turistada increible. Todo se ve mas que preparado. Es un saca dinero para turistas.  Carretera cortada. Un tejemaneje de motos para bajarte y si te descuidas te cobran mas que un Grab o taxi que vas comodo. Tienes que pagar 150.000 dongs por persona! mas las motos. Vamos de lo mas caro que nos ha salido de todo lo que llevamos haciendo hace dias.  Ademas sucio, lleno de gente...de lo peor que hemos hecho en Vietnam hasta la fecha. Como se ve en una de las fotos, las casas no son ni las de un pueblo de hace años, sino que lo van construyendo y pintando. En resumen, que no nos ha gustado. Nos esperabamos Sapa en general más virgen, más natural, y es un pueblo de montaña super turístico. Ademas cuando sales, un agobio increible taxi taxi moto moto.... chillando como locos entre ellos. Nos hemos agobiado muchisimo. Me ha recordado a los espectaculos de Port aventura pero sin atracciones. No volveria ni regalado

Le village est beau, les paysages sont sympa et les cascades cool, mais point en moins car les décors font fake, avec des tuyaux qui ramènent l’eau pour les cascades, les fruits et fleurs qui sont en plastique, et très gros point négatif pour les chevaux sur la rivière qui ne semblent pas être très bien traités. Le cheval avait envie de dormir mais était forcé de rester debout.  150k dong l’entrée.

لا تضيع وقتك انه لا يستحق ذلك،مكان عادي جداً بعض الشلالات الصغيرة جداً والكثير من المقاهي المنتشرة في القرية ومحلات التذكارات فقط لا غير وسعر التذكرة مبالغ فيه جداً بالنسبة لمكان كهذا ، قرية اصطناعية لجلب السياح اذا اردت حقول الارز اخرج من سابا ب 20 دقيقة وسترى القرى الفيتنامية الحقيقة وحقول الارز الجميلة والطبيعة الخلابة وكل ذلك مجاناً

On pourrait le renommer Cat Cat Instagram Village.  Tout commence par des boutiques souvenirs sur 600m qui vendent toutes la meme chose, puis vous arrivez sur le fameux villages qui n'est traditionnel que de nom. Comme pas mal d'attraction à Sa Pa, tout est prévu pour pour prendre des photos instagram. Sans parler des chevaux qui sont immobiles toute la journée pour que les touristes prennent des photos dessus. Leur etat font de la peine à voir.  Franchement pas les 150.000 VND les mieux dépensés de mon voyage.

150k l’entrée, ressemble plus à un parc d’attraction qu’à une vraie représentation des minorités ethniques. Cependant le cadre est très beau

Udając się do tego miejsca myślałam, że zobaczę realistyczny obraz  wietnamskiej ludności żyjącej w górach. Niestety jest to komercyjne miejsce, wybudowane na potrzeby turystyczne z dużą ilością straganów z produktami nie mającymi nic wspólnego z wyrobami handmade. Na duży plus są jednak widoki, które można podziwiać podczas spaceru. Dzięki nim zakup biletu w wysokości 150 tys VND od osoby, nie był tak bolesny.
Personalmente me pareció demasiado artificial, solo pensado para vender souvenirs a los turistas. Entrada 150000 vnd
園區維護差，木棧道破敗不堪，只有最下面拍照算還可以
이쁘긴한데 너무 인위적인분위기입니다. 하지만 조경을 위해 많은 노력을 한것같습니다. 폭포도 물을 끌어올려 만든인공폭포인듯하지만 괜찮아요
Plus touristique que ça tu meurs, je comprend pas trop la hype sur ce village. C’est sympas mais sans plus et bon pour 5€ l’entrée je m’attendais à mieux

No me pareció gran lugar.Excesivamente masificado y destinado al turismo(El camino está repleto de tiendas de souvenirs).La gente hacía más ruido que la propia cascada y la basura aparecía por todas partes.Deberían mejorar la limpieza del recinto y solicitar a los turistas un comportamiento más respetuoso con el lugar.Es una pena porque podría ser un lugar precioso en medio de la naturaleza,pero está explotado.
관광지 입니다. 실제 마을은 볼 수 없어요 ㅠㅠ
風景還是很美的 租車服務太坑人了 一直上下樓梯 樹屋還要另外付錢
15만동의 가치가 없는 관광지. 구글 리뷰를 믿었어야 됨. 호기심에 가고 후회하길🙏
Die Fotos anzuschauen hätte gereicht. Rein für Touris gemacht. Haben uns etwas anderes vorgestellt.
너무 상업화 되어 있어요. 입장료도 비싸고 돈내고 쇼핑하는 느낌. 라오차이나 타반이 더보기 좋습니다.

Der Ort ist wie „Disneyland“. Man muss Eintritt zahlen um das Dorf zu sehen (150.000 VND/Person), danach fühlt man sich wie hinter einer Paywall bei Temu/Ali/Amazon/Etsy. Das ganze Village besteht gefühlt nur aus Verkaufsständen und man wird sekündlich angesprochen was zu kaufen. Trotz bereits bezahltem Eintritts gibt es Bereiche, die extra kosten: „oh eine süße Katze - um dahin zu kommen, kostet extra“. Dann steile, ewig lange Treppen. Um wieder hoch zu kommen, darf man ein überteuertes Taxi zahlen…

Ich würde das Cat Cat Village nicht nochmal besuchen. Ist eine absolute Touristenfalle. Zunächst bezahlt man 150000VND nur damit man sich danach durch enge und überfüllte Treppen durchschlängeln darf. Ständig wird man von den unzähligen Verkäufern angesprochen und wenn einer der Touristen stehen bleibt, um etwa ein Foto zu machen, stockt alles. Will man sich mal abseits der Treppe etwas anschauen soll man extra zahlen. Ist man dann im „Dorf“ angekommen, gleicht das eher einem Themenpark (der auch gerade im hinteren Teil weiter ausgebaut wird). Wir waren zu Fuß zum Dorf gelaufen und weil die Straße ziemlich steil und durch den Verkehr auch nicht ganz ungefährlich ist, haben wir ein Taxi zurück nach Sapa genommen. Natürlich mit entsprechendem Preis.
비싼 입장료에 비해 별로 만족스럽지 못하네요. 소수민족마을과 사는 모습을 보고 싶었는데 기념품점과 관광용으로 만든 건물 위주라 많이 실망했습니다. 속살까지 보지는 못해도 자연스러운 모습을 기대하고 갔는데 그런 기대는 접고 민족의상 입고 사진 많이 찍으실거면 가셔도 좋겠어요.  입장료가 8000원 가까이하는데 저는 주변에 권하고 싶지는 않네요.

Masificado por turistas que van hacer su reporte de fotografía. Sí o sí pagas la entrada, es un lugar donde es todo pagar y pagar… sacar los cuartos a los turistas. Creo que hay más grupos étnicos a los que visitar…
村莊過度開發 梯田只剩一小部分 多項工程進行中 整片都光禿禿 裡面很多租借傳統服飾的店 即使租了 但背後卻不是美麗梯田瞬間沒意義 村莊店家很不友善 只有在要你付錢時會和遊客說話 失去村莊純樸風格
Rien d’authentique, il faut y aller en s’attendant à voir une "mise en scène"

Quel intérêt de payer pour visiter une galerie marchande ?! Une succession d’escaliers, et en bas vous pourrez louer des costumes pour faire local et vous faire prendre en photo. Le spectacle n’a pas eu lieu.
Katastrofa, jeden wielki bazar. To miejsce zdecydowanie jest przereklamowane

Leider enttäuschend. Es kostet ca. 4 Euro Eintritt. Dann wird man von Verkaufsständen überhäuft und dauerhaft angesprochen man solle doch etwas kaufen. Das komplette Dorf ist für mich nicht mehr traditionell, sondern auf den Tourismus abgestellt. Schöne Natur und an sich schöner Anblick unten im Dorf bei der Brücke. Trotzdem von mir keine Empfehlung.
상점이 많다. 비슷한 상점들. 포토존 있다.(꽤 내려가야 함) 다시 올라가기 힘들어 20~25프로? 올라오고 스쿠터 돈(25년 3월말 5만 동) 주고 타고 올라감.

5 étoiles: -1: 150k VND (6€) d'entrée -1: Les Vietnamiens qui klaxonnent en permanence, y compris dans le village. -1: Route très désagréable: camions, bus, motos, vendeurs, et piétons se bousculent dans une route étroite avec la poussière, de l'eau qui ruisselle, et des pots d'échappement. Motos partout même dans la partie payante. -1: Que des vendeurs de souvenirs. Aucune authenticité. -1: Malgré le prix d'entrée élevé, l'accès aux toilettes est payant (5k) Points positifs : +1: les cascades +1: le théâtre: qu'il faudrait mettre davantage en évidence par exemple en donnant le lieu et l'horaire des représentations à l'entrée... Total: 2 étoiles.
Im Prinzip zahlt man 150.000 VND um von allen Seiten angesprochen und angebettelt zu werden. Der Wasserfall ist schön, aber die ganze Village ist eher Abzocke... Würde ich nicht empfehlen!
Viele Läden, die immer wieder das gleiche Angebot haben. Die Spots sind aber schön. Wirkt wie ein Themenpark

Man bezahlt 150 000đ um an kleinen Läden, die alle das gleiche verkaufen, vorbei zu laufen. Manchmal gibt es checkpoints, wo man um weiterzulaufen nochmal 10 000đ Eintritt zahlen muss. Hat keinen wirklichen Mehrwert. Der Ausblick ist schön, wenn es nicht neblig ist, aber den findet man auch woanders.

Una lástima…  Lo que es el pueblo «tradicional» ocupa un quinta parte del recinto, el resto no es mas que un gran bazar reconvertido, mal cuidado y sobreexplotado.  De no ser por el precio de la entrada que para el lugar nos pareció abusivo (150000 dong/persona, +/- 6€), sería un paseo por el cual comprar alguna que otra especia, tela o recuerdo de la zona.  La parte tradicional, por ser justos, está mal cuidada pero hay una intención de preservar lo que un día fue una localidad única y culturalmente rica. El camino de salida, es lo que más agradable es, sin todo el bullicio de gente y la parte más salvaje.

Das Dorf wurde speziell für Touristen gebaut. Es wird gezeigt wie aus Hanfpflanzen über verschiedene Schritte Kleidung hergestellt wird. Außerdem wird regelmäßig traditioneller Tanz aufgeführt. Die Kultur und tatsächliches Leben wird nicht wirklich dargestellt, sondern eher als Schauspiel inszeniert. Kann man machen, muss man aber auch nicht unbedingt .

Überfüllt, tausende Stände die nur das gleich verkaufen und an gewissen Checkpoints wird nochmals ein Eintritt verlangt. Es gibt einige schöne Wasserfälle zu sehen aber sonst hat es mich wenig überzeugt.

Ein wirkliche Welt für den Tourismus. Für das Village weiter unten ist hier der Eingang, dabei wird ein Eintritt von 150.000 Dong (6€) verlangt, das war es uns nicht Wert. Wer es liebt überall angesprochen zu werden und sich jeglichen …
입장료 150000동입니다. SNS용 사진찍는 분들께는 좋을 것 같습니다만, 우리나라 풍광좋은 계곡을 도가니 헌납하고 가는 기분이랄까 ㅎㅎㅎ 풍광이 나쁘지는 않아요, 하지만 너무 덥거나 다리가 불편하신분은 구지 가지 않으셔도 될듯해요. 입장료에 비해서 별반이었습니다.
굳이..... 자연산 천계가 있습니다. 꼭 위에서 내려오시길...주차장에서부터 트램타고 가면.... 계단지옥이에요
그냥 우이동계곡+ 용인민속촌+전주한옥마을상점가.입니다 계단이 너무 많습니다 어른들모시고는 걸어서가지마세요 2주차장으로 가시는게 좋습니다 메인입구는 30분이상 계단 내려가고 올라가고~~ 와우~~~~
입장료도 있는데도 불구하고 볼거리는 거의 없었고 가축을 방목해서 사육하기 때문에 볼거리도 되긴했으나 더운 여름 비까지 내린후라 악취가 풍겨와서 유쾌하진 않았다
7년전에 방문 했을때보다 루트가 더 짧아진거 갔고(일단 오후 3:30쯤가서인지 모르겠지만) 그때 그 순수했던 모습보다 상업적인 모습으로 많이 변한거 같아 좀 실망스러웠다. 어쩜 처음오는 사람에겐 더 많은 볼거리 제공이라 생각 할수 있을듯

Arnaque !!! Village à 70 000 l'entrée les prix à l'intérieur du village sont multipliés par 2, obligé de payer 5 000 pour aller aux toilettes, n'y mangez surtout pas c'est hors de prix !! Le seul truc sympa c'est quand vous allez plus loin …

Ist ein absolut kitschiger Ort. Alles sehr aufgesetzt und überall Verkaufsstände. Wer entspannt rund um Sa Pa wandern möchte, kann getrost einen Bogen drum machen. Die Wasserfälle sind auch nicht der Rede wert. Dieser Ort wird Hauptsächlich von  Asiaten besucht, um Fotos zu machen. Eintritt zahlt man ca 70.000Dong.

Ganz nette Wasserfälle aber äußerst überfüllt und zugebaut, dadurch sehr laut und voller Müll . Außerdem ist fast keiner der sehr vielen Stände von Minderheiten sondern von Zugewanderten geführt. Die eigentlich öffentliche Straße gehört zum Tourismusgebiet und ein Ticket muss auch bei Durchquerung bezahlt werden.
Za 70 k je možno vidět uměle vybudovanou (nikoliv autentickou) vesnici. Pokud chcete na WC tak toto není v ceně vstupenky ale platí se dalších 5 k. Podél cesty samé obchody.

Das 'Dorf' besteht aus einer Reihe von Souvenirläden. Ansonsten gibt es nichts zu sehen. Dafür 150.000 Eintritt zu nehmen ist unverschämt.  "Ins Nichts kommen Leute Schon kehren sie um Ihr Geld bleibt hier"
원래의 마을을 보기는 힘들고 모두가 상인들 입니다. 피곤하게 강매를 하지는 않지만 피곤합니다. 계단이 너무 많아서 무릎이 안좋으신 분은 비추입니다.
Turistada por 6 euros. No merece la pena para nada. Gastate el dinero en un taxi a alguna aldea cercana para ver algo original, en vez de esto prefabricado
글쎄… 굳이 안가도 될듯. 일단 걷는건 힘들어서 시작점부터 오토바이 타야 되고. 표 끊고도 오토바이 타는거 추천.
Il faut payer 6 euros pour entrer dans le fake village.
C’è davvero poco di interessante da vedere, considerato anche il prezzo del biglietto (150.000)
Rempli de bordel qu’on peut trouver sur Amazon, il faut payer 150 000 pour rentrer, puis de nouveau pour aller dans certains endroits, c’est vraiment juste un truc à touristes
Massiv überrannt von Touristen, meiner Meinung nach nicht sehenswert
Si bien las cascadas son lindas de ninguna manera vale la pena el pago de 150.000 vnd
Décevant, va fait tellement fake, rien d'authentiques a mon avis
Terlalu artifisial
Todo artificial
Waterfall, the show and rainbow slide are worth it.. rest is pretty much just photo ops for those that dress up in the vietnamese outfits. Lots of stairs and walking. Some call it a 'tourist trap'
This is a good place to speak some time though not a real village but the scenic beauty of the water fall and a few cultural programmes makes it interesting one time visit is ok
Really ONLY made for tourists, I don't think you get anything from the real culture here. Yes - still kind of exciting but I wouldn't go again and pay admission 150,000 VND.

Total tourist. They charge 150,000 Đồng to enter an artificial village to dress up in a typical costume (price apart of course) and take selfies. They have taken advantage of the environment to manufacture a village with shops and restaurants.If you can do the trekking, better but, if you can not, it is better to go to the funicular (tourist train) to Muong Hoa station which has very good views of the rice fields.
A rather touristy village, but nice to look at. With some (many) stairs you can explore the village and the many shopping opportunities.

Cat cat village was part of our Sapa tour. It was very pretty but this meant there are lots of people trying to take photos. This made the place very crowded and underwhelming. We did a walk with the guide (just three of us including the guide) up a hill at the back where there were no people. We got to see water buffalo, the crop fields, growing indigo plant and a beautiful view. This walk was worth it and was a really nice break from the shops and the crowds. You will see the photos attached of this.There were some authentic houses where we learnt about the drying process if their food and the dying of there clothes using the indigo plant. There was also an old hydroelectric plant built by the French. Although this was interesting I would maybe give it a miss next time unless you want to get photos for social media I guess.

Soo many stairs .  Of slight interest good workout if you are prepared and interested in stair climbing.  A few lovely views / waterfalls,  but honestly the whole area is full of lovely views ,  lots of cafes where they will also serve you coffee,  no stairs involved.  Its a giant store on stairs followed by photo op (you can rent outfit get pictures, mostly young girls ) then short cut out ( more stairs) .

Flowers and waterfalls beautiful,  but felt like I was at a Disneyland set.  Cost was 300k vnd to visit, but nothing really cultural apart for many tourists dressed in traditional costume and taking selfies,  lots of shops, a couple of ladies weaving.  It was a downhill walk. Thankfully, you could take a "golf cart" for 10k back up the hill. An hour was enough, and we went around 430pm so a bit cooler.  Ticked the box, but wouldn't rush back.

Located about 15 minutes drive from Sapa town. Lots of Grab , comfortable & cheap. Entrance fee Vnd150k pp.Be ready to walk about , wear comfy shoes. Lots of restaurants & WC. Watch the cultural shows . Rent a traditional costume , take great photos. Another Vnd150k per costume .Lots of sorveniers n tit bits to buy. Its summer, so weather is hot about 25°Beautiful waterfalls . Place is well maintain . Spend about 2 hours there.

Cat-Cat Village is nice, but it doesn’t really feel like a real village.  Semuanya terasa palsu dan dibuat untuk pelancong. Kami suka budaya dan keaslian. It more like if marvel made a village, you can imagine how it would be. Actually you can visit without a guide ok. We went in very fight times and it must be much more beautiful in the sun. Even our day, it is very busy and feels commercial and not real.
A village built ad hoc, artificial.The first part is just Shopping but if you follow the path to the waterfall the landscape deserves , very nice to dedicate an hour

(Visit on April 12, 2024)Recently Cat Cat Tourism Village began popular, accelerated with the increasing popularity of Sapa. Many tourists that go to Sapa also visit Cat Cat Tourism Village. Unfortunately, Cat Cat Tourism Village is not for everyone.Tourist need to down the stairs just to visit Cat Cat Tourism Village, because the area was located at area with lower height than Sapa. The stairs itself have hundred steps. Too challenging for everyone whose have difficulty on using stairs. At some area, the stairs were very steep. Through the stairs, there are plenty shop selling souvenir (mostly locally made), craft, food and drink. Thankfully, tourist can return to above by using electric bus.At the location, visitor can see a beautiful waterfall and watch locals showcasing traditional culture. Well, it's really look neat, but need much effort to visit there.

Sadly this black Hmong village is now only a tourist attraction. You can just about imagine what life was like with the river flowing by and the ethnic group engaged in their typical activities. Of course life is no long as it was..poor and harsh.

Not bad - the waterfall and scenery are not bad. Just that the entire place do give me that it is man made. For the r first time visitors it is ok to go and visit as it offers nice scenery. It is good for people who can walk but elderly are best to avoid as there are many steps and i doubt the elderly can walk.

Cute village but touristy. Took a walk there from Sapa town and the views were stunning. Cost 150.000 for a ticket to go inside and they had shops and restaurants in the village. I ended up spending about an hour there including a stop at one of the restaurants overlooking the village. The waterfall was pretty as well, but nothing extraordinary - it all felt like it had been built up just for tourists to visit.Took a motorbike to go back to the town and I paid 70.000. It was a good day but don’t think I would go back.

We visited Cat Cat Village on a wet day, after touring Fansipan Mountain. What we didn't expect was that Cat Cat Village is actually a valley at the foot of the mountains.From the car park area we walked down a slope, then lots of stone steps to get down to the valley.My wife and I took a slow walk down hill. We were rewarded with picturesque views all round, gentle small waterfalls and a cool December afternoon.There are shops that rent colourful ethnic costumes to tourists, which made for a great photoshoot as a souvenir for your visit. It is a touristic place and I can imagine the crowd if it's the high peak season.We took a walk around the valley, to the bridge of the far waterfall and then walked back to the car park. The walking back was a tough one as we had to walk up slope!Overall I would rate this place a 3.5, unfortunately I can only give it a 3 or 4 rating here. We did enjoyed our time exploring Cat Cat Village.

If not for the ending area where there's traditional performance and the waterful, probaby would have rated as 1 star.Find it too commercialised.
