At the entrance after paying for the tickets, you will pass by a long stetch of shops and some photospots.For some photospot, it seems like you will have to pay a small sum, or buy a little stuff or drinks from the shop before you could take the photo.You will get a better deal if you skip buying your stuff from the earlier stall,  if not please do some bargaining. Note: this is a one way route. Right from the entrance to the ending where you need to get transport back by taxi, or by your tour coach. We had ask a staff at the shop who told us the other way. Even though we did use google translate to asked. Which was actually for scooter ride out only. Look out for sign "Come back", as a exit sign. Once you follow the "Come back" sign, hiking a flight of steep steps up then either you walk a while to get to the taxi area or could just ride the electric car. Cant remember how much we paid for.There's free washroom available when you see the village. This place might be tricky for who people who have knees problem. As there's alot of stairs.
Beautiful village with beautiful viewFor hiking for about 1 hourBut too many people in February - April.
There are MANY steps downwards and they are not that even. The waterfall at the end of it wasn’t that great either.I wouldn’t go out of the way for it.

The entry fee is on the high side. After entering the village, a long row of souvenir shops greet visitors. Entry fee is payable to visit the tree house. The waterfall area is fine. Folk dance performance is interesting. Advisable to take taxi back to town.
This Cat Cat village is worth for teenagers or someone like selfie and check in man. Visiting ticket is too expensive 150k/ pax. I am not interested in this attraction any more

Se encuentra a dos kilómetros del centro de Sa Pa, si el día lo permite, se puede llegar dando un paseo, aunque también puedes coger una mototaxi o taxi para llegar.La entrada no es demasiado económica, precios para turistas. El interior se recorre en un paseo tranquilo, cruzando por una zona de tiendas y souvenirs antes de llegar a la cascada.Importante iniciar el recorrido desde la parte de arriba, ya que las escaleras son muy angostas en caso de hacer el recorrido a la inversa.La cascada no es nada espectácular, pero el lugar en sí tiene su encanto. Muy preparado, pero merece la pena su visita por el entorno natural que lo rodea.

Lots of tourists.  Surprised at the more than 2x price hike as we relied on reviews from previous few years.This place will trap you right from the entrance with a slew of shops, all the way to the first waterfall.  Certain areas were poorly maintained.  Adventuring in the village can be challenging as the signs were not all that useful.  Be prepared for some steep climb.Would recommend if you have time to spare and not sure what else to do.  Otherwise, you can possibly get this view from other villages that are not as touristy.

Touristy spot, easy walkable and all spots are designed for capturing nice photos. Activities: watch dancing performance, how the local folks do textiles and handicrafts, mini shops selling souvenirs and snacks etc. Expect to spend about 1 to 2 hours: including walking and rest.
Not best time for hiking but we tried to visit the village. Low visibility, heavy mist, road from running water and unusually cold for April’s weather.

We come here not knowing what to expect, be sure to buy your ticket first instead of just walking in, the guard will point you to the ticket office. 150,000vnd ppWe saw some lively views here and had our daily cardio workout as very steep steps and dodging all the people posing for insta. The waterfalls are very picturesque and worth a look at I was a little underwhelmed but it was an ok experience, all the stuff they was trying to sell was expensive and looked like it had been made in china not locally.

There are lots of steps to get down. I hurt myself and was struggling to climb a few steps.Though it's beautiful to see, it's not advisable for elderly people and not advisable to those who have knee or back problems.I didn't have knee problems, but it occurred due to this walking.Please be aware of this

It was incredibly touristy, but what I found out from a local H'Mong guide was very sad ro hear:Apparently the local village leader tricked the villagers into selling all the land to a company, and although they are allowed to remain there for the time being, they no longer own their own homes, and could be asked to leave at any time as the company turns the village into some sort of amusement park.A very sad story.

Once you look past the fake touristy stuff, the gram-photo chasers, the items for sale from eBay... The natural beauty of the hills and river are spectacular. Walked down from terrace cafe and back again. We are certainly not fit hikers but in taking our time, it was completely do-able. The fog was super thick when we started our walk, but once we were in the valley, the most opened up and the view was beautiful. Definitely think it's worth the effort if you have time to spare and aren't in a hurry. If you don't mind giving in to harassment on your return trip, an eager motorcyclist will ride you back up the hill to Sapa for a fee.

Cat Cat Village at Sapa- Distance from Eden Boutique Hotel & Spa is less than 10mins - ard 2.1km via motorbike or car- Distance from Eden Boutique Hotel & Spa is less than 35mins - ard 2.3km via walking (Down slope all the way, hence I do not recommend this route for older folks)- There are many motorbikes or Buggy car at Sapa City to bring you down to Cat Cat Village- Entrance Fee is 70,000 VND (Adult) / ard USD3   30,000 (Child below certain height) / ard USD1.50- The tickets can be brought at the T-junction before heading into Cat Cat Village- Don’t try your luck by saving the entrance ticket fee cause there is a guard to ensure all that enter in has a ticket.- The Village itself is nice & can take a couple or more hours to complete it. Depending on your pace.- As we entered into the village is all the way down via slope & stair which can be very tedious & tiring - depending on your age & stamina - According to the local, the Black Hmong here in Cat Cat Village is not the authentic one.- The signage can be confusing so always ask & confirm the direction that you’re heading.- There are some undergoing road work along the path that is heading to the “popular waterfall / bridge & cafes” when we were there on 8th Sept 2022- Heading to the exit is equally exhausting due to the stairs & steep slope.Overall: It’s a nice place … something different from the usual. A lot of instagram shot can be take from here.

I thought it would be entertaining as a museum for the children at least, but even from that angle I found it dissappointing. All the comments about the beautiful landscape are hard to understand: the view to the few rice terraces close to the path is blocked by the stalls selling mostly chinese rubbish like furry toy cats. The two waterfalls are fairly small, the restaurant we tried did not have tasty food and even the dancers of the mini cultural show looked bored to death (exception granted for the flute guy). I regret not taking all the other negative comments / warnings on TA more seriously.
שווה להגיע לשם לביקור אומנם הכפר נראה כתפאורה של תאטרון אבל אין מה לומר יפה. אנחנו הלכנו ברגל  מסאפה זה היה ממש נחמד וכיפי לא קשה מדי. בחזור לקחנו מונית :) קיצר שווה ביקור.

CatCat village is a pretty place with lots of good views of the rice fields and mountains. It was pretty quiet when we went, but we weren't hassled to purchase anything. It is aimed at tourists, particularly those who want to pose in front of wicker hearts and dress up in traditional tribe outfits.

Bản Cát Cát là một nơi check in lí tưởng khi lên sapa, tới đây mang đậm chất dân tộc , có cho thuê trang phục để chụp ảnh, phong cảnh đẹp , tuy đi bộ có hơi mệt nhưng cũng đáng để có thêm vài bộ ảnh xịn xò

Visited this place after a looooong ride on the sleeper bus. The trek was easy but it was very slippery due to the rain and it's a cobble-stoned pathway. The village wasn't authentic to me. The guide said it was built as a tourist attraction. Lots of places for insta worthy shots and you can even upload it on the spot as there's wifi at the village. The view was not bad but would have been better if not for the rain.
I love the vibe. Walk around village and see the native show dance, drink coconut water, see the waterfall and make friends with local
Lots of photo opportunities, it just felt a bit fake. You can rent local costumes to wear whilst in there, it was full of people trying to get the ultimate selfie.

Per my understanding, local people no longer lives in this village, it was converted to a "live" museum for tourists. Some of the old houses are still maintained here with people stay to showcase their life before the conversion of the village. Most of it are just shops selling cheap mass-produced products. It is still an entertaining enough walk to the village, through it toward the waterfall. The waterfall is where all the action is, you can try watching a culture dance, join a local game, or simply walk around to take photos for your insta. If you are daring, you can rent the costume, made after local minority tribe's fashion and walk around.
Well, it is nice turistic village. I would not wisit it on more time. You can enter village from three enterences amd you have to pay enterence fee

Bản đã bị du lịch hóa nhiều rồi. Nếu bạn chủ yếu muốn đi xem, tìm hiểu về đời sống của người dân tộc tại bản thì sẽ khá thất vọng đấy. 2 bên đường đi xuống là các shop bán hàng, có các điểm để check in chụp hình, nhưng cũng không có gì đặc biệt lắm. Mình cảm thấy giá vé tham quan hơi cao. Ngoài ra, đi bộ nhiều, xuống và lên rất nhiều bậc thang, nên nếu người lớn tuổi sẽ khó đi và rất mệt. Tóm lại, nếu bạn chưa đi qua thì có thể đi 1 lần cho biết, nhưng để có ý định quay lại thì khó.
Vào đây chủ yếu là đi bộ đi bộ và đi bộ, trong bản này chỉ có 1 cái thác nước đẹp và không có gì nỗi bật

Sehr touristisch und künstlich. Eher auf asiatische Touristen ausgelegt, die verkleidet die Treppen steigen. Absolut auch ohne Guide zu finden, Karten gibt es vor Ort genug. Der Rundweg, den weniger gehen, ist gut in 2h zu meistern. Mein Tipp den Rundweg andersherum gehen. Dieses selbständige handeln verwirrt die Einheimischen zwar,  aber mit Beweisfotos dass man wirklich anders rum gegangen ist, kommt man auch am 2. Checkpoint vorbei ohne nochmal Eintritt zu bezahlen. Im Tal ist dann alles wie ein Disneyland aufgebaut. An jeder Ecke ein Fotomotiv. Die Natur ist nicht schön. Dafür lohnt es sich nicht hin zu gehen aber für einen halbtagesausflug mit Kindern ist es denke ich gut geeignet, da es nicht zu weit zu laufen ist. Zu unserer Reisezeit wären viele Läden geschlossen und nicht so viele Touristen da. Das war super!

Trocha už stráca autentickosť, moc zamerané na turistov. Pekne výhľady. Dole pri vodopád bývajú tanečné predstavenia. Predávajú tam nejaký odvar z medvedica obličiek-nas sprievodca neodporúča kupovať.

Werden vanuit het hotel geadviseerd om hier eens te gaan kijken om een echt Vietnamees dorp te zien. Daar aangekomen moet je al entree betalen om überhaupt binnen te mogen. Op zich is het leuk opgezet maar overal waar je kijkt zijn kraampjes met dezelfde produkten. Dit loopt echt giga lang door tot je in een dal bent en daarna weer naar boven klimmen. Snap dat mensen geld willen verdienen maar dit is te namaak
質の良いお土産屋さんは2.3軒ほどのお土産屋さんばかりが並ぶ村でした。時間帯によって黒モン族のダンスが見れます！　我々は往復タクシーでしたが、行きは徒歩で帰りはタクシーとかでも良いかもです。

Красивая река ступечатая и каскадная из всего остального сделали дисней лэнд. Сами украшения парка у реки мне не понравилились и деревня не настоящая. В противоположном направлении дервни красивые и настоящие, с большими рисовыми полями. А это развод для туристов. Если бы не река вообще бы пожалела потраченное время.  Тем более вход платный 😒 лучше сьездите в та ван, там настоящая деревня и правда красиво, а не это все🤔

Very beautiful scenery,  can't be missed. Oxen farming,  countryside. Can see the house of the ethnic minorities. And stone carvings, waterfall.  Wonderful experience to deal with the ethnic minorities,  they are very friendly,  is the art to communicate with them,  especially we can't speak Vietnamese.

Villaggio finto, pieno di bancarelle con venditori che continuano a chiederti di comprare e tutto il resto sembra costruito apposta per i turisti. Sconsigliato in caso di poco tempo. Decisamente meglio i tour organizzati con le guide nelle risaie.

I was thinking that Cat Cat village was an actual village, but it is really more of an attraction and market. First off, there is a fee to enter which was strange to me but I guess it supports the locals. I figured out later that the stores outside the entrance rent out the Hmong clothing for visitors to wear. As soon as you enter, there are many items for sale there including Hmong clothing. We bought some items to help out the locals. I didn't enjoyed being followed by ladies trying to convince us to buy their products. If you plan on visiting Cat Cat Village, be prepare to go down many many steps and then back up many many steps. Think of it as going on a hike with stairs. I would not recommend it for anyone with Knee problems.
I visited Cat Cat village, I don't understand what they do with the entrance fee. The maintenance of the place is not well.

We visited on a Saturday afternoon so no doubt an overly busy time. The walk down from Sapa is along a busy road. We paid 70k pp to enter and it would have been handy to be provided with a map that we later found others were given.Past all the local handicraft stores is a pleasant stream and waterfall. Time it right and you may get a show near the waterfall. We seemed to leave without getting the real essence of the place. Unfortunately it all felt a little contrived, in saying that still worth the visit.

ביקרנו בכפר קאט קאט שנמצא כמה דקות הליכה מסאפה. כניסה לכפר עלתה 70 אלף. לאורך המסלול(מעגלי, 1.5 ק"מ), יש המון חנויות שמוכרות שטויות לתיריים, פחות התחברנו. המפל למטה יפה ובדיוק כשהגענו התחיל מופע של ריקודים על ידי המקומיים עם התלבושות המיוחדות שלהם, זה דווקא היה נחמד -להעביר את הזמן לצלילי המנגינה המרגיעה ולהיחשף למשהו קצת שונה . בקיצור חוויה נחמדה, הייתי ממליצה רק אם אין לכם משהו יותר טוב לעשות. תהנו :)

Se vi trovate a Sapa un giro conviene farlo,anche perché ci sono dei punti panoramici e degli scorci bellissimi,tipo la zona vicino le cascate o i mulini.Nonostante sia un po' artefatto,consiglio di dedicare un paio d'ore.
Czy warto ? Nie do końca jestem przekonany...stragany i knajpy oraz kilka lipnych miejscówek ...poza mostem i wodospadem ...ale za to powrót bezcenny, bo już Taxi z graba negocjuje ..komercja
Les villageois font beaucoup d'efforts d'aménagement pour rendre la visite agréable, dommage que les nombreux chantiers de construction soient entrain de gâcher le paysage

There are interesting things to see and views of the rice fields, gardens etc. but it's also full of shops selling the exact same merch imported from China. I'm not sure why, but everyone is selling the same plastic disco-ball massagers and oversized flasks.I think you should go and take in the experience while you're in Sapa, but overall the village was not that great.

We did a 2 day trek through villages around Sapa and were disappointed by the tourist made element of this village. If you aren’t up for doing real treks it does give you a more accessible snippet of life here but is a glossy version. Interesting regardless just depends what you want.

Because i came from a country that had the same culture with vietnam, come to cat2 village no t difference than come to ethnic village in my country, the fascinating is we can rent ethnic local dress for 2 hours and walking taking picture with it

We stayed in Cosiana Hotel and decided to walk to Cat Cat Village. The road goes toward downhill direction, it takes us about 35 minutes to reach Cat Cat Village from Cosiana Hotel. We pay VND70,000 for the entrance fees to Cat Cat Village.Alone the trail through staircase are shops that sells traditional clothes, scarf, handmade bracelets and bags and again the staircase goes toward downhill direction to the waterfall and Butterfly Flower Garden, so it means I need to climb the staircase when I leave Cat Cat Village on the same trail. It's a challenge to me.While climbing up the staircase on our way to leave Cat Cat Village, we bumped into our tour guide who took us to Ta Van Village the day before, she asked us to leave Cat Cat Village through main road which is just next to Butterfly Flower Garden as climbing up the staircase is tiring.At the main road, a few motorcyclists offered to give us a ride at VND100,000 per trip, I shaked my head and the price went down to VND80,000. One of them, "Hey, it's s only 4 dollars". I told him, I am not from USA! I wanted to tell him, I am not earning USD, okay?! But I don't think he can understand what I meant. We ignored them and decided to continue our walk to Cosiana hotel and it took us 45 minutes to reach Cosiana hotel as we walked towards the uphill direction.We are satisfied as we made it, although we are tired, we take it as a good exercise.
Im Oktober eher nicht zu empfehlen da Reis bereits geerntet und sehr regnerisch und neblig. Das Dorf ist schön gestaltet aber sehr touristisch.
A place created for Chinese and Vietnamese tourists. From all places on the route the waterfall is nice.

Agree with most of the reviews. The village is beautiful by itself but toooo touristic not really authentic. Probably main attraction for locals coming to Sapa and they dressed like locals. Prices a bit higher due to the high demand and many visitors. There are few nice places for good photo shoots and we reccomend the colorful Lutus garden before going down to the waterfall.
הכפר מאוד מאוד יפה, עם נופים מדהימים ומפלים יפים. יחד עם זאת, מרגיש תיירותי משהו, ולא לגמרי אותנטי. אפשר לדעתנו לוותר על ההופעה המוצעת.
Very very turistic place. Many people come to visit specially in weekends. Doesnt feel like a real viilage. Many shops everywhere
A must see if you are at Sa Pa but go to Ta Van or other villages for authenticity.  Not far from Sa Pa and very interesting ethnic culture amongst lots of tourists.

So many tourist taking pictures with everything. The village is not as traditionnal than before. Like each village you have to pay 70 000dong per person to enter the village. Not much interesting hike. Would not really recommand for trek. Nice if you want to shop traditionnal items, sew by hand.

The valley looks to be rebuilt for tourists. The walking route is very narrow, so you cannot see the village itself and all covered with a small local staff shops with indifferent sellers ( that is good as the great you " hellooo, shop in my plaaace" with not even raising eyes ). Waterfalls are beautiful as not touched by people. Rustic WCs all over the valley at 5k dongs

An endless row of souvenir shops, hordes of ladies and children harassing visitors with cries of "shoppiiiing" and "buy for meeeee", this is a place I avoid like the plague. About the only good thing  I can say is that walking there and back is a good exercise. Much better villages to be visited.

Parece un escenario de una película. El lugar es bonito pero está tan turístico que pierde interés. La bajada hacia el pueblo está llena de tiendas que todas venden lo mismo. Y el recorrido completo lleno de niños explotados que no van al colegio. Una pena, es el precio del turismo.

Il villaggio è più che altro un percorso obbligato fra bancarelle che vendono artigianato locale, alcuni più originali e altri decisamente più dozzinali. Ci sono molti angoli selfie e tutto ha un po’ il sapore del “fatto per turisti”. È comunque meritevole di una visita anche se la vita nei villaggi è altra cosa.

Walked from Sapa centre to the road leading to Cat Cat Village. 3km. Cat cat is home of the H'mong people. The paved slope down towards Cat Cat valley was like descending the souvenir trail. Flanking both sides of the road were souvenir shops, local costume rentals and nick nacks. Don't empty your pockets here as this is just the beginning of a 3km trek. The trek subsequently narrows to a cobble-stone paved trail and meanders though the villages descending down to the valley floor. If you have difficulty walking down steps, this may not be a suitable walk. As we descend into the hillocks, it was like walking through  unending rows of hill tribe runned souvenir shops and houses. The children will shove the handicrafts at you or block your path... Plenty of local handicrafts, textile, pouches, bags, bracelets, wristbands, stone carvings, wood carvings, etc. Though I know the locals need to make a living, I was there to trek and not for shopping. Was one of the "longest shopping" village tourist attraction I have ever been to. Somewhat inauthentic, and taken away the innocence of the hills.

We zijn te voet vanuit SaPa hierheen gelopen. Dat is prima te doen en op zich een mooie wandeling. Op een gegeven moment moet je wel een toegangskaartje kopen, maar vooruit. Daarna is het nog een hele reeks trappen vol met toerische winkeltjes naar beneden, goede schoenen zijn aan te bevelen. Eenmaal in Cat Cat zelf is het een grote toeristische attractie, maar op zich wel aardig. Terug hebben wij een motortaxi genomen.

Tl;Dr:A quick and easy tourist spot with some nice scenary (bridges, waterfall) and lots of local folks. Not a must-see. Bring rain gear.Cat Cat village is a short 3 km walk from Sapa. There is a small entrance fee to get in (70k VND for adults, if I recall correctly). There are a ton of the local black Hmong ethnic people selling their wares, where the most interesting item to purchase are blankets/scarves/table runners made from hemp (marijuana plant).The village itself has a nice waterfall, some hydroelectric structures from the French colonization, and a nice river that goes through the middle. There were some other nice places to take pictures, but beware - you're going to have to fight hundreds of other tourists for the same places, so be prepared to be firm and keep your spot while you wait for the perfect picture.Honestly, it's a nice place, and if you have some free time, you should make a trip down here. Round trip, we only spent ~2.5 hours here, so it's a quick attraction. There's a good amount of hills, so bring some good walking/hiking shoes.There was also some rain - bring some rain gear. You'll be sorry if you don't!
A lot of walking in Cat Cat village, we enjoyed watching waterfall and culture performance. Must buy dry flower for tea making. Good buy.
판시판, 함롱산을 다녀온 후라 그런지감흥이 없었어요.온통 샵으로 꾸려진 마을 같았답니다.사이사이에 포토존이있었지만 그냥그랬어요....내려가는 코스라 돌아오는 길은 거의 올라가는길이라더위에 힘들었네유;;ㅋㅋㅋ마을에서 보는 뷰보다 마을 박 올라가는 길목에서 바라보는 풍경이 더 아름다웠습니다.

Had a spare hour so did a trip to Cat Cat village.   NIce to see the waterfall and the river at the bottom of the mountain.  Was hot.  There are places you can sit and have a drink or something to eat.

Sehr schön und sehenswert aber nicht barrierefrei. Ich musste mich sehr auf die Treppen konzentrieren. Frauen können hier für kleines Geld die Tracht der nationalen Minderheiten anziehen, damit im Dorf umherlaufen und für Fotos posieren.
We stayed at the aira boutique and managed to trek there in 30mins. Worth a look if staying in sapa. Lots of steps but having done fansipan the two days before, it was a doddle!

Jolie promenade jusqu a la cascade avec de beaux paysages montagneux.... Mais que de touristes... Et trop de faux personages etniques avides de vendre quelques souvenirs.. C est une deception de mon voyage.

We had some spare time so we went there, the entrance fee is 70k VND and first part is full of markets and then there is quite small part which should be like authentic, but no locals were there and just tourists.. definitely not number one to visit in Sapa:)
This once H’mong village is now a Chinese owned tourist trap. It is still very beautiful is you can get past all the little shops with the same old stuff.
It's a fair old hike out. Once there you'll go past many shops selling the same thing.... Pretty touristy and LOTS of climbing.Local boys have it sussed and offer you a motorbike to sapa.

Twee uur door dit 'dorp' heen gelopen. Een hele attractie, maar weinig authentieks aan. Behoorlijk smerig (wc's, veel plastic, werkzaamheden) en ongeïnteresseerde verkopers (merendeel met hun smartphones aan het spelen). Waterval was de moeite waard. Voorstelling leek ons ook leuk, maar zijn we helaas niet aan toe gekomen. Als je van souverniers houdt is dit een mooie plek! Verkopers waren niet opdringerig. Als je een aantal dorpjes verderop gaat (met gids of scooter huren) zie je veel meer van de streek. Voor de entree (70k) een OK attractie.

Not much of the real village exists as it is overrun by tourists. The location is pretty and there is a nice waterfall with shops and eateries overlooking it. Then again similar sites exist in many countries. I found the amount of rubbish strewn around the place rather disappointing though. Getting there does involve a couple of hundred steps so not suited for anyone with limited mobility.

מדובר בהליכה לתוך הכפר עם מדריך, במקום משפחות עם ילדים שעוסקים במכירת מוצרים שהם מכינים.כדאי לרכוש ממתקים ולחלק לילדים הקטנים.מקומות צילומי נוף מדהימים, כמו כן מגיעים למפל.הרבה מה לעשות אין, עוד מקום לעשות וי בביקור בסאפא.
The scenery from the village worth the visit And the payment of 70,000 dong. On the trail some shops and cafe. The area sorrounding the waterfall is extreamly touristic and artificial.
It’s more like a tourist place than a common village. Full of tourists, and shops. Walking to a waterfall in the village is recommended.

Cat cat village is a village built for tourists.  There is a 70,000 dong entrance fee and then you descend many many stairs to get to the bottom.  All the way down are stalls selling same same.Once you get to the bottom it’s quite scenic with a waterfall and some water wheels.  It’s then quite a steep climb back up.

Le seul intérêt d'aller dans ce village fabriqué,sont la promenade et l'environnement.Car, depuis Sapa jusqu'au village, ce ne sont que des boutiques de souvenirs.Les locaux se déguisent en costumes traditionnels, un petit spectacle médiocre.A faire quand on n'a rien à faire d'autre.
Lots of stairs going down and up. Shops were lined alongside. I dont find this attraction that interesting. Mostly man made structures. Great hike and work-out for the stairs though.

Very touristic but still has some interesting things to see. Our guide showed us all sorts of things. Lots of  vietnamese tourists as well dressed up in local tribe costumes.  If you can walk 10 to 15 km, then better to do trekking to other villages for more authentic sights.

Although the interior of the village was aesthetically pleasing (bridge with beautiful flowers as well, small waterfalls and water wheels), it gave off a very man-made vibe and seems to be created for tourists. Restaurants look underdeveloped though.
... ormai oggi è un luogo troppo turistico, forse in passato aveva il suo fascino, oggi è un luogo molto pacchiano, con buona pace per coloro a cui piace

Sono ritornata da una settimana dal viaggio in Vietnam durante il quale ho visitato anche Sapa e vari villaggi.Ho meditato molto prima di scrivere questa recenzione perchè mi rendo conto quanto sia  pesante....E' facile raccontare solo il bel paesaggio...ma ci sono anche altri cose meno belle...Bello :  I terrazzamenti  per la coltivazione del riso sono fantastici considerando la quota di 1500metri. Anche le persone sono bellissime nei vestiti tradizionali e i bambini meravigliosi.Perplessa :  l'ingresso ai villaggi è a pagamento, questo è giustificato dal fatto che i denaro raccolto viene reinvestito nei villaggi...quello che però personalmente non riesco a capire è la sporcizia disseminata per terra, nei ruscelli e nei terrazzamenti. Bottiglie di plastica, buste di nailon, scarpe, stoffe...di tutto !!! Se si ritiene far entrare i turisti in una zona protetta,(visto il pagamento del biglietto) come un parco,   perchè nessuno si impegna per pulire e raccogliere lo sporco ?  Le guide dicono che ci vuole pazienza e che prima o poi le amministrazioni se ne prenderanno cura !! Un'atra cosa che mi ha veramente sconvolta sono i tetti della case che a me sembrano di ETERNITH. Le persone ci vivono e ci sono anche B&B per turisti. Alcuni di questi tetti sono rotti e scheggiati. Anche le nuove costruzioni vengono coperte con questo materiale che la guida ha definito di "fibra di cemento".Non sono un'esperta, e quindi mi scuso se sto dando informazioni sbagliate, lo sto facendo in buona fede,  quello però che mi preme segnalare, non solo ai turisti, ma anche alle guide turistiche e alle agenzie turistiche locali, che se per caso il materiale di copertura è veramente di etrnith, questo è altamente cancerogeno.

Cat Cat village, being about 2.4km away from Sapa town, is one of the easiest accessible villages on foot - an easy downhill walk all the way from town. However, because of its proximity to town, it's also loaded with local tourists dressed up in gaudy factory produced "Hmong" costumes, posing everywhere and taking selfies to show off on social media. The village is located in the valley, so there's lots of stone steps to walk up and down as you navigate around the area. As much as I try to like this place, it feels as though this village has been turned into a tourist trap and the Hmong locals who live there are the exhibit. Snap a photo of a Hmong child dressed in traditional garb and they come running after you to sell you a trinket or two. Do yourself a favour and head out to Ta Phin village or Ta Van village instead for a more authentic feel of rural life.

We walked through the village with our Sapa Sister tour. It was touristy but beautiful to see the crafts of the Hmong people. We were fortunate to watch traditional dancing by Hmong dancers.If you have the time walk through.

This village is for tourists. It may have been authentic at one point (?!) but feels very much for show now. I was in Sapa for two days and there was thick fog, so I wasn’t able to go on a long hike like I had hoped. You could barely see 20m at times! So visiting Cat Cat was pretty much my only option - and although not ideal, I AM glad I did visit... It was my only real chance to glimpse terraced rice fields and bits of local culture. Note that there are many tourists, including some groups that are dressed head to toe in the local clothing and posing for photos / selfies. Feels a bit bizarre and discourteous if you ask me!

It is difficult to be kind to Cat Cat. Overwhelmed by huge numbers of tourists,mostly Vietnamese.Don't even consider going at weekend or public holiday.Take a taxi or motorbike to the pay booth (about 100k dong).The entrance is slightly uphill after buying tickets.The approach road is steep and narrow and choked with vehicles in the morning. This place is NOT for the elderly,disabled or infirm visitor,DON'T EVEN THINK ABOUT IT.the path downhill after the entrance is rough and difficult.It is lined either side by market stalls selling traditional wares to the point of saturation.There are few remaining traditional buildings.There is a nice flower garden with diffcult access about half way down. Reaching the bottom you can further descend to Cat Cat waterfall and river village down several hundred steep steps.This is by far the nicest area and worth the descent,There is a beautiful  waterfall and a smaller onefurther upstream.Many old village equipments are located down here,some pretty bridges.There are some cafes and restaurants and good quality toilets.The river is extremely dangerous as is wading inthe water without shoes.Be VERY carefull with small children.There is no safety supervision at all. Many Vietnamese nowadays chose to rent hilltribe costumes for their visit making a colourful scene . To return you have no choice but to hire a motorbike otherwise it's a real climb back to the entry.

It’s very touristy, with small shops every were, that’s made for tourism. But it is still a nice walk, with some beautiful views. There wasn’t so many people when we went. It can be a long walk, if you choose to walk back up. Might as well take a scooter, they will be there.

Worth a visit to get the flavour of a mountain village. Women like to hire mountain costumes to fit into the scene and there is plenty of street food to experience. Could not help but feel sad for the number of children not at school.It's a steep descent to the stream and with the bridge, is a great place for photos. We climbed a short way up the other side and got a motorbike ride back to the town - best decision!
실제 사람들이 살고 있지만 방문객들의 방문이 많아지면서 거의 비슷비슷한 기념품들만을 파는 가게들로 전락한... 다소 실망스러운. 하지만 시간없는 이들이라면 이거라도 보세요.
The place at the end is a local market they sell something everywhere. Quite nice view, few interesting things, good walk, but surely you don’t fell the experience.

Cat cat is a fake village set up for tourists ( vietnamese and foreigners). After walking down the road and paying an entrance fee, you end up in a shopping trap where locals try to sell you the same « hand made » stuff that you find in any souvenir shop across Vietnam. Then you go down slippery, unstable and very busy stairs to end up in an instagram village where the main activity seems to be taking selfies in front of seemingly authentic houses, water wheels and bridges. Nobody actually lives in this village, the children are there for display dangling jewelry or keychains for sale in front of you while their mothers look at you with dewy eyes. Then you take the way back with more shops and climb along a busy road, being passed by honking motorbikes and polluting cars, being splashed by muddy water since there are no pedestrian sidewalks per se. Now, it’s still an ethnological experience to see so many tourists renting traditional costumes and umbrellas so they can take pictures of themselves in front of a fake decor, the natural scenery is actually beautiful ( beyond the setup), some locals put on a good show of traditional dances, it is a good workout after ingesting so much deep fried food like the delicious nems. So if instagram rules your life or you are capable of irony then why not giving it a try?

The walk down hill was a bit scary with all the cars and bikes beeping and zooming passed. Once down hill, you are thrown into a one way tourist trap where the kids are being used to sell merchandise. Yea, it may be for a good cause but it made me sad to see. The trek out was definitely for the “In shape” traveler. But you do have the option to grab a bike on the way up. It was enjoyable....as I look back on it...now comfortably in my hotel room lol.

An easy walk downhill from town, although a bit of a grunt to climb back up. 70000 dong entrance fee. Very touristy, but a nice area to stroll around. People aren’t into being photographed unless you are buying something.
I spent more time to see the waterfall, long time to walk but surrounding is the cultural of Mong life, some part is very dangerous because of rainy season.

Went to Cat Cat Village is now very aimed at tourists so you don't get to experience the authentic hill tribe villages. The waterfall was amazing, and the lovely lady in her little shop on the way to the hydro electrics was a god send for a sit down break and an ice cream.

It’s a lovely area, lots of stairs and a nice area at the bottom of the stairs near the waterfall. On the way down the stairs there is just stall after stall selling gifts and kids coming up to you trying to sell bracelets. It’s a bit of a shame but is still a nice area to visit for an hour or two. It’s 70,000 Vietnamese dong each for entry, less for kids. But if you go past where there’s space to park bikes and follow the road down the hill and then left you can get in without paying and miss out most of the stairs. Unfortunately we found this out after we’d paid but it isn’t too much, about £1.50 each.
Het is een erg toeristische plek met heel heel veel verkoperstentjes waar je langs moet voordat je bij het dorpje komt. Het dorpje zelf is leuk, maar niet erg bijzonder

Theo lịch trình khám phá Sapa đoàn tôi đi xe đến bản Cat Cat, đi theo bậc thang xuông thác nước, bản cát bây giờ có nhiều nguồng quay nước và phong cảnh vườn hoa hai bên đẹp, bạn có thể theo một vài bộ quần áo các dân tộc để chụp ảnh,

Sapa đã thay đổi nhiều kể cả bản cát cát, trước kia mộc mạc thơ mộng, nay đã xây dựng quá nhiều và quá đông đúc; ở nơi đây có phục vụ ca múa nhạc dân tộc và mặc quần áo dân tộc chụp ảnh cũng rất thú vị
상업적으로 마을을 꾸며 놓은 느낌. 상점이 즐비하다. 하지만 사파 중심지에서 걸어서 얼마 안멀기 때문에 접근성이 좋다. 몽족들이 사는 모습을 제대로 체험하기엔 무리가 있지만 가볼만하다.
사파역에서 걸어서 도착. 이미 마을 입구는 차량, 오토바이, 사람으로 난장판. 표를 가지고 마을로 감. 많은 계단 옆에 업소. 계단을 내려가야 마을이 나옴. 안개로 볼 수 없는 마을 전경. 폭포의 웅장함. 다시 와야 하나..
베트남의 민속촌을 온 기분이었습니다워낙 유명해서 방문했는데 비수기라서 그런지 기대만큼 아름답지 않았습니다동양인 대상 보다는 서양인 대상으로 호객행위가 많있구요, 대부분 전통의상 입고 사진찍는 사람들이었습니다.입장료 내고 채험하기는 soso한 곳이구요. 출구에서 바이크 흥정 해서 싸게 타고 가시거나 택시 타고 가세요. 올라가는길 힘듭니다.
사파가 한참 공사중이라 걸어다니기 불편하고 먼지때문에 힘들었고 사파에서 가까운 깟깟마을은 걸어가기 충분한 위치에있으나 특별하게 볼것은 없었고 우리나라 둘레길 걸어가는게 훨 운치있었음
Wow. This village is beautiful and was a wonderful experience. A lot for steps to walk down and up and not for the faint hearted or people with physical restrictions.
Nice but very touristy! Close to Sa Pa, you don't need a guide to get there because it's easy to find!
It’s a touristy place but still cute with a lot of up and down (stairs). We enjoyed hiking from cat cat to the next villages definitely more.

Está muito montada para turistas! É uma descida e tanto! Tem um rio que passa na aldeia e tem uma cascata bonita mas vê se que perdeu alguma identidade e é um cenário montado para turistas! Mais como um museu!!

Wenn man vorher ganz normale Bergdörfer besucht hat, kommt man sich hier vor, wie auf dem Rummel. Sicher ist es schön angelegt und es gibt einen schönen Wasserfall. Aber leider wird man, wie auch in Sapa, ständig von Frauen bedrängt, etwas zu kaufen.Zur Attraktion wurden für uns dann schon eher die Vietnamesen, die aus dem ganzen Land anreisen, um sich in gekauften oder geliehenen Trachten im Blütenmeer und vor dem Wasserfall fotografieren zu lassen.

Si vas hacer un trekking perfecto , pero si solo vas a verlo no pierdas el tiempo  es un parque de atracciones para turistas , todo está totalmente colocado estratégicamente para que compres además los vendedores ambulantes te acosan para que les compres o hagas un trekking con ellos. No volvería ahhh y en noviembre el arroz está recogido así que ven en otra temporada a lo mejor mejora ?¿¿?

Es ist ehrlich gesagt ein Touristenpark, wo sonst bezahlt man Eintritt. Von authentischen Dorfleben weit entfernt. Wer aber noch Souvenirs sucht oder einen Spaziergang auf einem gepflasterten Weg unternehmen möchte ist hier genau richtig. Es ist alles etwas kitschig für meinen Geschmack aber es gibt Leute die stehen da drauf . Viele leihen dich fort die typischen  Landestrachten um in der Blumenwiese oder im Wasser Fotos von sich machen zu lassen, ok ich gebe zu Europäer hab ich keine gesehen die das gemacht haben. Aber auch so gibt es an der einen oder anderen Ecke mal ein paar schöne Fotomotive. Wir waren zu zweit mit unserem Guide dort und haben durch sie ( hat übersetzt )dann auch ein bisschen was über das einheimische Leben und das Handwerk erfahren.

The promise is to visit a typical village but you only find a lot of shops for tourists and a lot of people around asking themselves what are they doing there... All look artificial and you only want to get out as soon as possible. Some details are nice but in general I don't recommend this visit
Ei kovin erikoinen turistipyydys. Tekemällä tehtyjä rakennelmia, jotka tuskin kiinnostavat monia. Tuntuu olevan kiinalaisten ryhmien suosiossa.

Certes nous l'avons visité un week end par temps brumeux voir pluvieux et des centaines de touristes locaux mais Cat cat est un village "fabriqué" pour les touristes asiatiques avec location de costumes traditionnels, décors de photos.  Pour passer le temps....

Llegamos a Sapa a mediodia y no daba para un trekking muy largo, así que escogimos este de mediodia a Cat Cat, con visita a todo el village.  Hay que pagar entrada, y hay todo un recorrido circular.  Hay xeom para volver casi desde cualquier punto, si uno se cansa.  El recorrido tiene puntos bastante bonitos.

Walked down to Cat Cat from Sapa, which is about 3km. A good afternoon hike if you want to do something without a guide. Lots of cute handicraft stalls, good views and a nice waterfall at the bottom. Worth being aware that it's quite a long way down and can feel like you'll never get there. Easy to get a taxi back to Sapa at the end if you want. Beware of water buffalos on the path!

Since we had a half day before taking a mid-afternoon bus back to Hanoi, we decided to wake up early and head down to Cat Cat Village before the "crowds" do. It is relatively easy to walk down to Cat Cat (we did it in 35 minutes) and we believe you can see the village without a guide, but if you want the knowledge of how and why the Black H'mong live like this then sure, get a guide. The price to get into this village is 70,000 Dong, which you can get at the door. The village in the old part is pretty, but there are a lot of shops lining the route selling souvenirs, so I can see why people mention this is "souvenir village". Since it was quite early (08:30), the villagers weren't in the mood yet to sell. We continued down to the Tien Sa waterfall and the beautifully preserved watermills. This is probably the highlight of Cat Cat Village as well as the bridge that you see at the end of the route. Otherwise, I would say there isn't anything special about this place. The treks to the other side towards Ta Van or to Fansipan mountain are more exciting. Yet, like I said earlier, this is a good option for a half day (2-3 hours) either on your first day or last day.

This is a destination to visit when come to Sapa. The entrance fees is about $4. The village describe a normal life of local people with traditional yields ( bags, shower,foods, souvernirs) especially brocade. You can find something for your family here. One tip for foreigner, the most famous food in Sapa is dry-buffalo meat but don’t buy here. Its not delicious even its cheaper than outside

Thought Cat Cat Village was going to be a genuine village of one of the minority groups in this region but turns out it has been built specifically for tourists - it has never been inhabited by the Mhong people, who actually live up in the hills. Even the water buffalo in the village looked like they had been trained to stand still and have their photo taken with tourists. It’ is kind of quaint and there is a lovely waterfall down in the valley but it left me feeling a little disappointed.

There are many nice artificial constructions. They sell many interesting stuffs. Visitors can learn briefly about ethnic people's lifestyle. It's like a place you must visit, but once for 4ver. Entrance fee: 70k VND/adult.Caution: stay away from local children and old people!

Een leuk dorpje wel heel erg toeristisch.We hebben het dorpje met een gids bezocht, als je alleen voor dit dorpje gaat is het niet nodig om het met een gids te doen. De bewoners proberen van alles aan je te verkopen, kreeg er een beetje plaatsvervangende schaamte toen ik daar liep. Chinezen zijn er dol op, lopen er rond in de lokale kleding die je daar kunt huren.

Cat Cat Village is a small, very commercialised village to meet tourist requirements. It tries to show traditional ways of living.There are a few houses showing traditional layout, tools, how to make cloth, cloth dying, music and entertainment, mills etcAdvantages: 1. Only a short walk from Sa Pa so easily accessible2. Set route on which to walk as built for tourists3. If limited on time can whizz through this to get a flavourDisadvantages:1. Pricey for what it is2. Very commercialised and busy3. Natural views of the rice paddies are very sparseIf you have time, would advise instead to go to another less tourist village, travel further or go to Lao Chai Valley. The mild trek there is great fun with spectacular views on the way

Fue el inicio de nuestro trekking con una guía local, hay que pagar entrada, la cual no es cara, no recuerdo el precio, pero podrás disfrutar de unas vistas bonitas y sacar unas buenas fotos. El punto negativo es que está muy orientado al turismo, lleno de puestos para que hagas gasto y de niños vendiendo pulseras
Place where we can see beautiful scenery of SAPA but filled with shops selling same things. We spent 2 hours in the village and some spots need to wait because too crowded with people.
2 ק"מ מסאפה בירידה, כדאי להגיע לשם ברגל ולחזור במונית. המחשה של כפר ויאטנמי, מפלים מרשימים. המון חנויות מזכרות .סיבוב של שעה
September is not the best month to visit. Very rainy, we got ripped off. And do not count on great food along the way. It is a very long walk from sapa Center. Would not recommend.
景色一般，梯田规模很迷你，也很原生态，我们是骑摩托车来的，摩托车路线和徒步路线不一样，景点比较少，全是坑坑洼洼的泥巴路，比较考验骑摩托车水平，喜欢骑摩托车的话还是别有一番风味的，离沙巴城非常近，时间充裕的话还是可以去玩一下的
Es bonito, pero artificial. Pagas por entrar en un centro comercial bonito, pero wue nada es autentico, o solo algunas casas aisladas.

Arrived late to hotel and guide was there to drag us off on the trek. She ran off so far ahead we couldn’t stop for photos and if we did she would push us on. It was all rushed 2000ft down hill on rough narrow tracks or roughly constructed steps constantly looking at your feet trying to keep your balance. Tried to look at the little stalls and what they were offering , but constantly told by the guide “ don’t buy it’s from China”. When we did buy some items she continued berating us for buying them. The whole tour was very rushed and the guide disappointing. The only thing that saved it was the views, the local kids running around and the other tourists in the group. And to add insult , at the end the guide said she would take us to her village the next day where we could buy the same goods.
Mọi thứ đã thương mại hóa, không còn cảm nhận nét đặc trưng của đồng bào dân tộc.Thất vọng. Không muốn quay lại.

It was something to do - nothing amazing but not terrible either. It's all very contrived and it shows on the faces of the people who inhabit it.  We preferred doing our own made up tours around the area than the group tours or this village.

There are many shops along the trail. We didn't manage to catch the cultural show. They could have better displayed the traditional house and way of living of the tribe. The hydrostation they mentioned on the brochure also couldnt really be found. There was a signage toward the end, but nothing looks like hydrostation at the direction they pointed. The only catch for me to visit the place was the waterfall and river.
Mooie waterval, wel erg  commercieel. Weinig authentieks aan. Wel schattig dat de varkentjes hier lekker los door de straten lopen. Waterval was groter dan verwacht.
호객행위는 지나친다해도 끝없이 마주치는 어린아이들의 내민 손은  끝이 없이 달려듭니다. 힘들게 내려가서 오토바이 타고 오는 인당 10만동의 비용이면 시내에서 렌트해서 타고 내려와서 다시 몰고 올라가는 것이 더 좋겠더군요.  내려가는 길에는 늘어선 상점들 밖에 없어요...  중턱의 카페에 들러 경치 감상하는 것이 외려 나은 듯....

Village very close to Sapa, really just part of Sapa "suburbs". Packed with tourists, pretty valley with waterfall. We started a 17 km walk through here, once we left the village, pursued by 3 women trying to sell us trinkets, the walk was beautiful, and worth the 30 minutes going through the village. There are a few things worth seeing, like the production of hemp fabric and Indigo dyeing. A local guide helps.

Though this village is much more touristy compared to the other villages in Sapa but you get some good shots here. It is a 3km downhill trek to the waterfall from the centre but the walk back is very steep and difficult and there are some people offering you a ride back from the waterfall but at a very dear price.
A very commercial village in Sapa compared with other villages. The waterfall is worth to see. If you prefer more nature views, I will recommend other villages.

Esta junto a Sapa. Tras sacar los tickets, empiezas a bajar por una empinada escalera, llena de tiendas a ambos lados, artesanía, ropa, nada interesante... puedes meterte en alguna casa y echar un vistazo.Mas abajo hay un mirador, con hermosas vistas...Algunas personas vestidas con  ropas étnicas, luego sigues bajando en dirección al rio, allí hay un puente y algunas norias hechas expresamente para que se hagan fotos los turistas.Hay una cascada rio abajo, muy fotogénica.En una de las casas hay danzas y bailes típicos.Luego subes por el lado contrario del valle y te recogen en coche o vuelves al inicio andando, que no son mas de 10 minutos.Vale, para pasar un rato.
do it but dont spend much time there. very touristic. sceneries are nice but you can find them elsewhere

Like many other reviews there’s a lot of very touristy things here at Cat Cat village which means you aren’t seeing an authentic Hmong village. That said if you want a good walk and want to see some really stunning sights right by the Sapa township then this is the go. It’s a solid 20 minutes walk down the hill from Sapa to the entrance and then another 20 to the bottom of the village where you can see the waterfall and other aspects of the village life. The walk back up will take an hour or more and is very steep - a good way to work up an appetite! Not far from the bottle. You can get taxis and rides on the back of scooters, all are expensive relative to regular services but that’s the price of avoiding a tough walk up the hill.

Cat Cat village is literally “village”. But I wonder whether it is a village. It is too much touristy. There are so many souvenir stores rather than local houses or cultural things. What I could see was many similar hand-made bags or bands. There are some begging kids. They seem just 5~6years old. They are trying to sell small stuffs to tourists. Wish all the kids to be protected and have rights not to work. Cat Cat village is very near from Sapa square. It is worthy to visit. On the way to Cat Cat you can see very terrific view. What I expect is that a village must be a village.

I wish I went to one of the other villages... I was very surprised at how touristy this place was and quite defeated the aim of going to a local village. Ask your hotel for suggestions of more native areas than going here.
In the middle of everything so beautiful (and this is also beautiful) it is like one big giant gift shop for tourists, pretending to show what life is like in the area.

Franchement je ne m'attendais pas à ça en arrivant à Cat Cat qui n'est autre qu'une sorte de village témoin pour touristes. Il y a des cascades en bas du village, et la prononciation de ce mot français avec l'accent vietnamien à donné le nom du village "cat cat". Alors on se ballade tout le long du village (qui soit dit en passant est propre comparé aux alentours, touristes oblige) à la découverte des maisons typiques des ethnies du coin, leurs façon de vivre, de travailler etc.. C'est un peu déroutant et parfois dérangeant car les gens vivent vraiment là et ça fait un peu voyeur à mon goût... Étalages d'artisanat et nourriture tout le long. Malgré cela c'est très joli et ça fait une belle balade surtout une fois que l'on a passé le village et que l'on se retrouve un peu dans la nature. Cette n'est pas le genre de visite que j'apprécie le plus, ce n'est pas de l'immersion, c'est plutôt voir à travers une vitrine. Je sais que l'entrée est payante mais je n'ai pas vu le prix.

We walked down to the village and the view was stunning. However after entering, we got more and more disappointed. Tourist shop after tourist shop. Again and again.The villa eis not a village anymore, it has simply become a shopping area with a few traditional houses here and there.The river area is beautiful but again, over developed with wooden bridges and swings etcIf I could do it again?I would skip it completely and take a car or longer trek to further villages and areas.Simply not worth it.

The sights are amazing, but there is someone trying to sell you something constantly. Mostly trinkets, but a few interesting things like woven items or knives. Fun to walk down to. Not as much fun walking back UP to Sapa!
Shop after shop, they commercialize this lovely village. Anyway, I love their traditional houses, very unique. Have some lovely view of rice terraced fields and surrounding.

E' abbastanza turistico ma non così eclatante da perdere la sua suggestione : questo villaggio è a 3 km. circa da Sapa, e gli acquedotti in tubi di bambù che forniscono acqua al villaggio nonché i grandi vasi di tintura blu (necessaria per gli abiti tipici) contribuiscono a renderlo meno "turisticizzato" e piuttosto caratteristico. I manufatti sono ancora artigianali e vale la pena acquistarli.

Il faut être clair dès le début, le « village » de Cat Cat n’est plus du tout un village. C’est maintenant une zone entièrement dédiée aux touristes, aménagée de manière assez jolie. A bannir pour ceux recherchant le « typique ». Nous avons fait trek jusqu’à Ta Van, beaucoup plus intéressant de ce point de vue.

You need to be fit to walk the big steps in Cat Cat Viilage.Typical of Vietnamese shopping there are 300 shops which all sell the same stuff. Lots of children and older ladies of the Humung tribe putting the hard sell on you as you navigate those steps and trying to catch your breath.When you finally get down to the bottom of the village the walk seem worth it.At this point take a good rest as the walk back up into Sapa is a long winding one.My tip is after the long bridge back their will be lots of bikes that charge a small fee to get you back to your hotel. Their are small taxis as well so don't freak and don't see them you just have to walk another 50 meters around the corner a bit.
A bit too touristy and busy for our tastes. You have to run a bit of a sales gauntlet each way too. It was great to watch local children playing in the water.
길 양옆으로 소수민족 공예품점이 끊이질않는다. 먹을만한것도 없고 공복에갔다가 다리아프고 포기할 뻔함. 물레방아있는 곳만 한번 이쁘다고생각하고 나머진 별로.소수민족이 애기델꼬나와서 파는 꿀 깍지도 않고 사왔는데 꿀이 시고써서 못먹음.

Beautiful village, but it has commercialis. So keep it mind From Sapa to you can get motorbike to down, but before you go, agree the price.If you there, watch there traditional dances in their theater
Wij vragen ons af of mensen hier echt wel wonen, lijkt een beetje een pretpark. Wij waren vooral geschokt door de uitbuiting van zeer jonge kinderen, die  daardoor niet naar school kunnen gaan.
The place is beautiful, but the touristy vibe is a little over the top for me. I recommend other villages personally.

Eine Mischung aus vietnamesischem Disneyland, Bauernhofmuseum und Souvenir-Großmarkt. Dazwischen kann man aber auch noch authentische Fleckchen entdecken. Etwas nervig sind die Frauen, die einen ständig zum Kaufen animieren wollen. Landschaftlich sehr schön gelegen. Das Ganze ist Geschmacksache.

เป็นที่ท่องเที่ยวที่สวยงาม แต่สถานที่เป็นภูเขา จึงเหมาะสำหรับผู้ที่ยังแข็งแรง สุขภาพดีเท่านั้น เพราะต้องลงเขาก่อน พอลงไปล่างสุดแล้ว จึงเดินขึ้น ระหว่างทางก็มีวิวสวยๆ ให้ถ่ายรูป มีคนพื้นเมืองมาขายของอยู่ข้างทาง มีบริการให้เช่าชุดชาวเขาใส่ถ่ายรูปด้วย อากาศเย็นถึงหนาว แล้วแต่เดือนที่ไป

It was a nice glimpse into the H'mong people who live in/around Sapa, Vietnam. However, most of this village is set up for tourists. Including the artificial village along the riverside. I am sure there was a village there at one time, just that the one we saw was so contrived. I mean it was replete with a mountainside small waterfall, but look a little closer and you'll see that water is coming out of a pipe. Travel a bit further from Sapa and you'll find better spots to visit.

The weather was kind when we were here - it didn't rain.  We were overnight in Sapa and three first day was a walk down too Cat Cat village with a local guide. The general view was amazing - steep cliffs with terraced rice fields but the village itself was touristy and overrun by tourists.  The walk is steep downhill but it a sealed surface with lots of steps.  It felt like we were on a tourist treadmill. There were some glimpses of real life (we were showed inside a house where a family currently live) but for the most part there were a lot of shops and people wanting to sell food, clothing or trinkets.  It was very manufactured and we got a more real slice of village life on our trek the next day.  It was a hot day and our guide arranged transport to take us back up the hill and i recommend that.

We're told that this village is in the few ones which still keep their tradition in Sapa, and it made us feel interested to visit. The natural beauty inside this village is truly worth to visit at the first place. However I felt that this village has become 'industrial'. There're too many houses offer foods & drinks, and they kept asking us to try. I didn't see many truly local activities there but just mini 'restaurants'. Furthermore, the kids kept following us to ask for foods and drinks, and it made me feel a bit uncomfortable during the visit. But in overall, it's still worth to experience one of the traditional villages in the North

A good walking track down the hill. Many people. Very crowded. A lot of shops, most of them are identical. Way back up the hill is a little bit difficult. The site is interesting for youngsters, for adults it's just a walk and shops.

The Cat Cat village have good scenery and view, yes it's a local village but there is a place in village i attached picture below, it's not truly make from local people. It's made like an attraction for tourism.

We travelled in late December, so we could only see about 10-20 metres in front of us, so the rubbish dumping was a bit of a non issue for us.  The walk is beautiful, if you can ignore the other kazillion tourists who aren't scared to shove you out of the way along very steep stairs and paths.  You get that everywhere, though.The village itself is obviously fake, but you can imagine how it must have been in the past.  It is just a snapshot of village life for the Black Hmong people, with a few interesting demonstrations to watch while you're there.  A good short walk to get you warmed up for the bigger ones through the hills and ankle deep mud of the outer villages.
If you have ran out of other things to do in Sapa then Cat Cat can be fun but it's a fake village but it's still fun to see how it used to be.

Nous sommes allés le matin marcher entre les villages de Lao Chai et Ta Van et l'après-midi au village de Cat Cat. le matin, dès que sommes arrivés, six femmes du village attendaient auprès de la voiture. Elles nous ont accompagné une bonne partie du chemin sans nous donner de dire non, car elles se sont collées à nous. Malgré cela, tout le long du chemin la conversation était agréable jusqu'au moment de nous quitter ou elles nous ont énormément pression et pour acheter leur marchandise. Ca a ete un moment désagréable, car, même si on s'en doutait, l'attitude était beaucoup trop insistante et les prix complément démesures. La marche est sympathique malgré cela et tres facile (pour tous les niveaux). On a passé à travers deux vrais villages à côté des rizières, ce qui est beau à voir. Par contre, il faut payer 75000VDN par personne pour entrer au village, ce qui ne nous a pas semble normal. L'après-midi nous sommes allés au village de Cat Cat, où il faut aussi payer une autre de 70000VDN. Nous avons juste faut le début du village (il était tard), mais nous avons été déçus, car c'était uniquement de boutiques pour touristes dans des marchés assez glissantes. C'est dommage, car l'endroit est magnifique, mais le tout est un attrape touriste, c'est qui est très décevant.

Cat cat village is located in Sa Pa and is designated as kind of a heritage village where the Hmong people continue to live like they used to many years ago. While I am for keeping their heritage alive, the place is very much commercialized. Goods sold are supposedly handmade by the Hmong people, but for the well travelled those can be easily found in markets in many south east Asian countries. Given that there is an entrance fee to the village, I was disappointed by the thrash littered all over, from the valley side, river streams, and streets. Nothing seems to be done to clean up the streets. I can understand that in the past people throw their stuff into these places, but those often are organic and will break down with time. However, the modern day litter of plastic bags and the likes are not going to breakdown fast enough. Whether it is tourist or the locals who contributed to it, the fact that nothing is done to clean them up is indeed a shame.

Came here for visit as part of our 2nd day in Sapa, here is what we think:Ambience: 4.5/5Cleanliness: 4/5Physical Ease: 2.5/5Fun: 4.5/5Value for Money: 5/5The Good: Very rich in local culture, a must come, very interesting to see how the locals do things and their daily livelihoods.The Bad: We came during Dec, and it was misty, making it hard to walk around cos of the slippery paths. The Bottom Line: It’s a must come, worth it to venture into the mountains to come see this place, highly recommended!

H'mong guide met us at our hotel and it was a 30 minutes easy down slope walk to a man-made Hmong village showcasing the usual stuff like their housing, weaving and other workshops with a backdrop of the natural rice terraces and a waterfall within the village.  Plenty of shops selling their ethnic produce.  On the return leg, beware that the walk is uphill and would take approximately 2 to 3 hours to get back to the centre of Sapa. Of course you can opt for a short taxi ride back for VND100,000.

We went free and easy, using just Google map to show us the way. Not a particularly difficult hike except for some parts when we took the extended trail which had larger, steeper steps. Doable with just sports shoes, but expect them to get rather muddy.Cat cat is actually quite a pretty village, especially when you get to the quieter areas but for the most part, expect tons of villagers lining the cobbled streets selling the same things. The hike can be trying if you have weak knees as the whole area requires you to climb up and down steps.
Went there in Mid Nov, weather is cold and raining most of the time. Path is wet and muddy. but overall a nice place .

Considering charges for tourist attractions in Vietnam (consistently 30,000 - 40,000 VND), Cat Cat entrance fee is very high - 70,000VND.Worth it?  Well if a significant portion of that fee goes to the villagers, then perhaps okay, but there are still lots of shops/stalls to earn income from.So, overall, it's an unreasonably  high fee for what;s on offer.To be positive, it's a beautiful place, especially down by the river where there are the landmark water wheels. Back to the down-side unfortunately.....coming back up from the river and waterfalls is VERY hard work! This place would be unsuitable for those with mobility difficulties.Final note for solo travellers......You'll be pestered by countless offers of "Motorbike?" "Motorbike to Sapa?"As tempting as it might be on the return, bear in mind that these are unlisenced 'cowboys', not genuine 'Moto-taxis'. That means no insurance. If you were to have an accident with one of these guys, your travel insurance would not cover any treatment you might need, and the driver won't have cover for you anyway!

Due to the growth of & tourist popularity of Sapa, Cat Cat has become the victim of its close proximity to the city. I imagine that it was once charming & rustic, but no more. The steep walk down into the village, surrounded by tourist shops is a turn off.But, once down below things get interesting - a weaver’s home, shaman’s house, then a waterfall, bamboo bridge & warewheels - all neat.Instead, try to visit other outside Sapa villages.

The village is basically just shops after shops after shops. Not much authentic things to experience. The little waterfall, streams, and the people there are what impressed us the most. Drinks and food are pretty expensive compare to other villages. Should get ready to walk up and down the hills ( many many stiff hills ), some has stairs, some does not.Better bring your own drinks, because paying twice the price for a bottle of water is not really worth it. While you already pay 3$ just to walk around all day.
Lieu agréable pour se promener lorsque l’on séjourne à Sa Pa, mais le village a perdu de son authenticité à cause des installations touristiques...

Pas très réussi l attrape touriste mais bon si ça peut aider les hmongs , petite ballade sympa au bord de la rivière qui aurait être plus bucolique si la pollution était moins présente , A mon avis pas d un grand intérêt

Nice easy walk down to the village if you have no mobility problems.  Found it interesting but has become very touristy and a little fake.  Many children begging which our guide told us has become a problem as they skip school to beg. Please do not encourage them rather donate stuff to the local school to help.
cat cat is very much adapted to the tourists except for the main street which is steep and muddy. plenty of opportunity to buy the usual tourist things
너무 호객행위가 심합니다. 적당히도 아니도 싫다고 하는데도 계속 따라옵니다. 너무 불쾌하더군요. 하지만 캇캇마을 밑의 폭포와 경치는 불쾌함을 조금은 불식시켜줍니다. 폭포 옆 건물에서 소수민족 전통 공연도 있으니 한번쯤을 볼만합니다. 여기가 끝이니 계속 걷지 마시고 다시 돌아서 올라가시면 됩니다.
Sympa mais sans plus. Hyper touristique. Pas très authentique. les villages ruraux valent davantage.
a very disappointing tour to the village.  There is no cultural value as its a open shopping market with very commercial touristy things sold.
Meget turister sted. Nærmest et natur Tivoli. Det går ud på at få solgt en masse til turister og der er ikke ret meget autentisk tilbage

The wander through the village is really just a wander through a souvenir market. There is all sorts of tat to buy or you can dress up in local costume to have your photo taken.A number of the homes are open to peak inside but no information is given unless you are with a guide. When you reach the bottom there are some nice waterwheels (just decorative), the waterfalls and to a schedule traditional dancing.We wandered back passed the lily pond and along the rice terraces. This was nicer and more authentic than the village. It would have been nice to see the harvest but by 1st Nov this was all over.The climb back to the top is a little step and muddy in places, at one point you have to keep recrossing the stream.

Wir waren an unserem Ankunftstag in Sa Pa dort, der Weg dorthin ist einfach zu finden und zu Fuß absolut machbar - eine geführte Tour nicht notwendig. Das Dorf selbst ist nicht mehr wirklich ursprünglich und total überlaufen, überall sind Verkaufsshops. Wir waren nicht begeistert.
เหนื่อยยยย มาก บันได หลายร้อยขั้น บางจุดชันมาก ทางเดินไกลมาก เล่นเอาหอบเหนื่อยจริงจัง มีร้านค้าตลอดทาง โดยรวมเดินดีเอาบรรยากาศ ได้อยู่ครับ

It is very easy to walk from Sapa to Cat Cat Village. Despite the fact that this place has become touristy, there are still some things to explore such as the view, the waterfall, the people and the dance. The ticket is around $3.

If you're expecting a fascinating insight into village life in the rugged mountains of North Vietnam, this is not the place to go.To be fair, Cat Cat Village is situated on the main road about 30mins walk (3km) beneath Sapa, so it's hardly isolated, but the rag-tag shops selling generic tourist tat that you find all over the country, and the fact you have to pay just to walk through what is a pretty unfriendly and over-visited town, makes it a rather unappealing experience. There's a few water wheels and a waterfall that are worth a stop, as well as a reasonably high rope bridge that the kids will enjoy, and a walk down here is better than spending a day in Sapa.Taxis charge a fixed fee to drop you off at the entrance and then pick you up at the exit immediately after the rope bridge and drive you back up to Sapa - worth the price as it's a fairly steep hike in the never-ending rain...
如果是中国游客的话时间紧的话这里可以跳过，因为国内这种村落和云南的梯田比这个要好多了。村里全部都是在卖手工艺品的店铺。

Easy walk down and around. River and old water wheels nice.Catch a scooter ride back up the hill!Bit touristy in the village part  but we did it because could walk fron Eden hotel. Pleasant if not too hot.

There are nicer places to visit around Sapa that don't charge 70k entry fee per person or feel so set up and staged for tourists.That said, if you have a free hour or two its worth visiting, but not worth going out of your way for.
A good walk down past the river where you can see all the old water wheels and how the rice flour was made, in all a good day out.

We came here with our guide as part of a tour. She described it as very touristy, which is a pretty fair description. We did take some nice pictures here, though and enjoyed our visit as a starting glimpse into Sapa village life.

This village is all set up for a tourist stroll, along a beautiful water system with a waterfall. There are loads of tourist shops set up along the walk , with heaps of steps. Good for an afternoon stroll, but not something to replace the real thing, a trek through a real village.

It was a nice afternoon walk. We started this walk with a big mistake. We did it backwards so we had to climb from the bottom of cat cat village to the top. At the top of the path there is a HUGE sign saying cat cat village. Don't miss this sign although it looks like a tourist trap. You must go down this path or you will be climbing all the stairs back up to the main road afterwards.The village is really a series of shops set up selling lots of indigo dyed objects and some silver jewelry. There are repeats of many of the "handmade" goods and you can tell that they've been sourced from a wholesaler. However the silver is usually handmade locally. There is a little wiggle room for bartering, but some hold fast to their prices.After you exit the shops area you will see the small waterfalls and the water mills. It's interesting to see how these could be used to power things, although it's obvious this has been setup for the tourists. You'll pass a bridge and then more shops. Overall it's a nice afternoon stroll with a few options for food in the middle of the stroll. However don't expect it to be an authentic local experience.
เป็นหมู้บ้านชาวม้ง ภายในหมู่บ้านมีการปลูกข้าว ทำนา ขายสินค้า มีน้ำตก และ รำธารไหลผ่านหมู่บ้าน เดินชมไปเรื่อยๆในหมู่บ้าน แต่ทางออก ต้องเสียค่ามอเตอร์ไซค์ กลับมาที่จุดเดิม

This village is not that authentic. They turned all the houses or place into a gift shop or restaurant to cater to the many tourists flocking to the village. This place has lost its authenticity and it is too commercialized that I got turned off. This is the nearest village to Sapa Town - I heard there are other villages more authentic than this but they are about 20km away.

Bản gần trung tâm nên là sự lựa chọn phải đi ngay. Tuy nhiên ở bản thì mùi khá là hôi. Tuy nhiên xuống phía sâu thì có thác nước và cây cầu dây treo khá là thơ mộng và mát mẻ. Khuyên mọi người là nên đi giày dép thoải mái, tránh tuyệt đối giày cao gót hoặc giày búp bê nhé. Mình thì phải mua ngay một đôi dép tổ ong để đi được thoải mái hơn và cũng dễ lội suối nữa.

Cat Cat est un village entièrement fabriqué pour les touristes. Nombreuses boutiques de souvenirs made in china etc...Cependant la balade est sympa et offre de beau points de vues. À faire en deux heures s'il reste du temps à passer sur Sa Pa.

Cat Cat est un petit village de boutique pour les touristes Une activité qui est tout de même sympas a faire pour les jolis paysages mais il ne faut pas venir a Sapa que pour cela car  manque d'authenticité.. Permet tout de même de ne pas rester à l'hôtel si l'on a quelques heures a occuper :)

chúng tôi lựa chọn cát cát vì nó là bản gần trung tâm nhất so với nhưng bản khác. đường vào bản phải đi bộ khá dài nên chọn giày or dép thật thoải mái. 2 bên là nhà truyền thống của người dân tộc và những quán bán đồ lưu niệm cho thuê trang phục dân tộc. phải đi đến cuối bản mới có thác nước đẹp để chụp ảnh.

Das Dorf ist ca. 45 min. entfernt von Sapa und ist ein. Kleiner wanderausflug für ca. 1,5-2std. Leider sind auf dem Weg rechts und links lauter Touri-Stände. Fande es trotzdem wert dort hin zu laufen für einen kleinen Ausflug.

The Cat Cat village is a pleasant walk from Sapa, it did feel like a bit of a tourist trap with many stalls pedaling similar stuff to the stalls in Sapa however it was a good way to spend a quiet morning. You can get a taxi back from the bottom of the hill but the walk isn't too strenuous.

Cat Cat is only a few minutes by taxi from Sa Pa, and the impression given by drivers is that they drop and wait.  Fare was negotiated down to 60,000 VND, just under A$4.00.We were dropped at the ticket office and told an hour wasn't nearly enough, and an hour and a half would be better, so we took the driver's advice, he gave us a business card and told us to call when we were at the pickup point.Right from the start, it's a tourist trap of junk shops, lining both sides of the walk in places, and mostly selling the same touristy things, the things that can be bought in Sa Pa.The scenery is spectacular, soaring mountains, deep valleys, and a steep, paved descent to the bottom of the valley and the water wheels.  The climb then starts back to the pickup point, where there were multiple taxis waiting.We asked a local to call the taxi for us, which he did, wouldn't take anything for the service, and our taxi arrived in about ten minutes.Worth seeing, but the constant, "You buy something from me" does become tedious.

Very beautiful views at this village. Everyone is very lovely too also however if you buy from one they all hassle you and say to buy from them too. Prices are very hiked up so say you already bought if not want to buy.
very commercialised. things for sale everywhere. having said this we were also in a rush and only did a small section of it because we had two young children with us. didn't do the entire village

Sapa is so amazing and there are many walks or rides you can do to see it's amazing beauty. Cat Cat Village has been set up solely for tourism. It's has traditional dancing and traditional homes set up for you to see how the people live. But it appears they have been created just for the tourists. Most of the village is tourist shops. They have a small fabricated waterfall there and some displays set up demonstrating how fabric is made by woman.I would only venture here if you have nothing better to do. The day before we went here we did a 12 km walk through Sapa hills which was amazing, and made Cat Cat a let down. Cat Cat was included in our 2 day tour.
This place is too commercial. No major attractions. There are better place to visit in Sapa. Shops tend to sell same things.

It's obviously a "no win" situation.  You have a pretty spot, with a lovely valley, river and waterfall and you have lots of willing tourists.  So you channel all those tourists down some steep, picturesque steps lined with hopeful vendors of cheap and nasty souvenirs.  Then you build structures from the abundant bamboo across the river with chimes and water-driven models to give those tourists something to look at.  We took one look and headed in the opposite direction into the most exquisite valley I've seen for a long time.  Hence this review gives an Average rating rather than Poor as we didn't complete the tour so I'm giving this "attraction" the benefit of the doubt!

Feels like something that has been set up for tourists. The water wheels are nice and opportunity to see local crafts being made.  Maybe go early to avoid crowds. A lot of stalls to buy. Wouldn't say this is a must do but if you need to kill time it will do

I take the tour to Cat Cat village by myself. The sightseeing on the road was beauty. I interesting with the daily life of local people. But the village were crowned with many tourists and some house on the village have a staged I don't think they poor (just my opinion). So if walk around it good, buy the tour you should considering.
This place is quite touristy but still worth the visit. You can buy different kinds of handicrafts made by the local tribes themselves.
Is nice place to visit but is too commercial as alot of shop selling the same product along the trail
Aardige watervallen. Proef Vietnam! "Zie en voel" de mensen! Leer het echte Vietnam kennen! Koop hier uw souvenirs, goedkoper in Vietnam kunt u het niet krijgen en heel veel is handgemaakt.

Lots of stairs, lots of stalls selling goods, some nice waterfalls, even some food and beverages along the way to relax. You should try the fried and dried jerky, very tasty. The rest of the village seemed to be mostly a little walk.

Leider hat das Dorf mit einem authentischen Dorf ethnischer Minderheiten nichts mehr gemein. Wer aber ohne viel trekking eine Art Freilichtmuseum besuchen will, ist hier richtig. Dafür ist es nett gemacht, wir hatten nur etwas anderes erwartet.
over commercialised and not many unique features that will make the visit special, the tour guide was friendly.

An easy walk down hill from town and although a bit touristy it was stil worth a look. We then ventured off the beaten track to a waterfall and area to jump off rocks into the river for a swim. Very refreshing !!

I would recommend to visit Cat Cat if you have little time to spend in Sa Pa or if you are not prepared enough for or do not feel like a trekking in the mountains. The spot is very 'tourist made' (some people may like it other not). Take a way that goes above the village and you will have no people plus amazing views.

A visit to this village is a shopping goal. To get to the waterfall you must pass a long street of shops with the same stuff you can get anyware else (only the guy with the  handcarved stoneware makes it himselve). The waterfall is nice but the village itselve is not outstanding.

It is a nice village and pleasant to walk, but as I read from another review it looks like Disneyland. Some stuff doesn't seem to belong originally to that place and looks kind of fake. Nevertheless is worth a visit, but there are much smaller, yet traditional and authentic villages spread by the mountains.

The scenery around the village is stunning, but the man-made tourist village that requires an entrance ticket, feels a little too 'touristy', artificial and crowded. It’s worth a look if you love photoshoots, but not a must-visit spot.

We visited Cat Cat Village after seeing a lot of posts on Instagram and were really excited to explore it. But in reality, it felt more like a touristy and commercial place rather than a truly historic or cultural village. Many of the …

Bản đã được tu sửa đón khách du lịch rất đẹp. Điểm trừ là cho thuê quá nhiều quần áo dân tộc không đúng và không phù hợp với văn hóa của địa phương. Khiến nhiều khách NN đến và hiểu nhầm đây là 1 dân tộc ( …

I didn’t like that you have to pay an entrance fee to get there. And once you pass the gate, there are lots of shops with vendors calling out “sir/madam, shopping, shopping.” All the stalls sell the same things. To be honest, I might have …

Be prepared for a very long walk up and down lots of steep steps. It is not wheelchair accessible, not recommended for those that are not able bodied. The steep steps can also be slippery when wet.  Plenty of stalls selling various home …
Entrance ticket 150.000 VND for foreigners (Mind the long stairs to access the village) …
Cũng không thật do chỉ dựng lại nên ko tự nhiên như bản làng. Khách chỉ thấy được phần nào về văn hóa.
