Only redeeming factors are finding cheap knock-off North Face jackets, and people-watching at night with a "fresh beer". Be VERY careful with aggressive scammers roaming the streets, especially the shoe repair guys. One grabbed my foot and started putting glue on my sandal, then took it off and started stitching it without any consent from me, then demanded money!

Yeah not much going on here . Full of tourists all wandering around waiting for something exciting to happen , you are constantly harassed to buy bits and pieces of mass produced rubbish , everything is a rip off or con of some type . The food is pretty good though and offers some relief with an awesome array of restaurants. A lot of trendy coffee shops , bars make it feel way to much like home so its a mega fail . You will pay the tourist price so be warned . If shopping for viddles and drinks back at your Hotel go to the big super market and pay around 50 cents for a can of beer etc instead of a whopping $1.50 on the street . The best bit about Hanoi was getting out of the  Old Quarter it self and out to the suburbs for a more authentic Hanoi experience where the food is real and the locals dont see you as a dollar sign . Ive traveled to Vietnam twice now , my first was in 2012 and then it was an amazing authentic place with a trickle of tourists , now its just a throng of Holiday makers swimming in a sea of sharks . . Respite is often found in a Pagoda where you can pay homage and breathe for a moment or two . 3 nights and 4 days is enough and by that time you are feeling burnt , financially and physically .
Busy, business, bars, scooters, traffic and nice restaurants. There are street signs but you can get lost. Just ask for directions

Non discuto assolutamente le loro abitudini , ma parliamo della giungla più totale , per strada non puoi camminare perché a quanto pare l'obbiettivo sono i pedoni ma nn puoi camminare neanche sui marciapiedi perché è un posteggio per i motori, assenza di semafori e mercatino all'aperto, peccato perché è un passaggio obbligato
오토바이가 많이 매연이 심해서 기관지가 안좋은 사람이라면 권하지 않음. 그러나 베트남을 간다면 한번쯤은 가봐야할 곳. 사람도 많고 오토바이도 많으니 안전을 스스로 챙길 것.
The old quarter is mostly wholesale shops selling packing materials and cellotape. You also need to be very mindful of the bikes as there are virtually no footpaths to walk on.  It's quite stressful.
When I got toread about this, I created a totally different inage in my head. The old quarter is just full of motorcycles all thre time. Not much atenthic souvenirs neither.

After visiting the old quarter in Hoi An we were expecting a quaint area to easily walk around.Not so.The area is frantic, not much old about it, with average shops and not as many bars/restaurants as we would have hoped for.

I made a list of all the best food stuff I wanted to try, ie best Pork Bahn me, best coffee, best pastry , Best PHO (fur).all but the Pho place was easy enough to find, we did it by foot walking around the lake from the French quarter. whatever you do if you get lost anywhere in Han oi, dont ask any males for directions , we did this a few times and then realised they were all laughing "at us" as we walked in the opposite direction. suffie to say we were too nackered by 5pm to do the food tour for another 5 hours. Pork Bahn Me was the only one on my list that was ok.

どこの国でも、市場やマーケーットは必ず立ち寄ります。地元の人ま混じって地元の物を買うのは楽しい物です。ですからとにかく行ってみましたが、あまり欲しいと思う物はなかったです。子供服やら防寒着、祭祀に使う金ぴかの飾りなどや安っぽいアクセサリーといった物でしょうか。お土産になりそうなバッグやポーチなどは売ってなく地元の人達の生活用品が中心でした。でも、生活を知る為には1度は足を運んでみても良いと思います。

Interesting, but not much more than that.In general bad service in the shops.Mopeds on the sidewalk everywhere will force you out into the street, and with the traffic in Hanoi it's at the risk of your life.Nothing really charming about it at all.
Noisy, dirty and not worthy of more than a taxi ride through it unless your aim is to bargain shop, visit the night market or walk your way to St Joseph Cathedral!

갑자기 생긴 짧은 휴식시간을 활용하기 위해 하노이 여행을 하게 되었습니다.숙소선정은 네이버 블로그 글을 읽고서 구시가지 중심가에 위치한 골목에 위치한 호텔에 묵었습니다.첫날 오후 무렵 방문한 구시가지의 모습은 아수라장을 방물케 했습니다.무질서한 차들과 무수히 많은 오토바이들,,그리고 아무렇지도 않게 무단횡당을 일삼는 관광객들과 현지 주민들...그리고, 아무렇지 않게 음식점이나, 호텔, 및 길거리에서 담배를 피우며 다니는 많은 사람들....물런 길거리에 많은 노점상들과 관광객들로 이색적인(?) 분위기를 연출하고 있지만, 가족 여행객이라면 매우 위험한(차량, 오토바이, 담배,,등등) 지역이라 생각 합니다.구시가지는 배낭여행객들과 젊은 여행자들에게 양호하시는 것이 나을 듯 하네요.저희는 4박 5일간 하노이에 있으며, 하롱베이와 하노이 이곳저곳(수상극, 맛사지, 등등)을 둘러 보았는데,,사실 어느것 하나 하노이 만의 특색을 찾아보기란 힘든,,,애매모호한 여행지 이였습니다.

수많은 오토바이와 사람, 자동차가 아수라장을 만들고 있지만 그 나름의 질서를 만들고 있는 것이 상당히 흥미롭다.처음에는 길 건너기조차 쉽지 않다가 점차 적응해나가는 자신을 발견하게 된다.하지만 수많은 오토바이들이 쉴새없이 경적을 울려대며 부딪힐듯 스치며 지나가는 모습은 마지막까지 적응이 되지 않았다.복작거리는 도시를 피해 여행을 떠난 입장에서는 무조건 좋기만한 기억은 아니었다.

O país possui tantas coisas lindas para ver que eu acho um desperdício de tempo passar muitos dias nas grandes cidades, sempre tumultuadas demais, pouco acolhedoras. Eu não achei muito interessante, achei que poderia ser mais limpo, pois a quantidade de ratos nas ruas me incomodou. O trânsito intenso e a falta de espaço nas calçadas faz a caminhada ser estressante. Dois dias é mais do que o suficiente para ver a cidade!
バイクの往来が激しい。散歩しているとバイクの排ガスで具合が悪くなってしまう。ベトナムなのに欧米人がいっぱいいる。
I found this place very busy with thousands of mopeds, and quite dirty everywhere. The pavements are difficult to walk on because most of them are damaged.

... but not all experiences are for the better. The Old Quarter should really have a renaming to the the 'The Unhygienic, Filthy Vehicular Death Trap Quarter'. From the moment we entered we were dodging oncoming traffic, whether they be motor vehicle, motorcycle, velocipede or pedestrian. The lack of adherence to basic road rules/laws is incredulous and I've no idea how we didn't witness any significant traffic crash instead of the minor bingles which were plentiful. The entire area is unsafe for walking and only slightly safer by vehicle. When you think you are safe dodging cars and mopeds you almost get electrocuted by the loops of power cables hanging around waist height. To be honest, though, electrocution and 'close bitumen contact' would probably be preferable to the numerous scammers and con artists walking the streets. Everything from the super glue wielding 'Shoe Repair Guy' to the pineapple carrying 'Fruit Merchant' seeking money for photos.Now, amazingly, none of these negative attributes compare to the stench. That gut wrenching, eye watering, nostril stinging stench that is the Old Quarter. To somewhat quote a colleague of mone, it's as if the whole of Hanoi suffered severe flatulence and the releasing aperture was located in the centre of the Old Quarter.Thankfully our hotel was amazing and it truly was our sanctuary.

If you go to the old quarter be wary. I have been scammed several times in the same day:1. Make sure you ask the price of stuff well before committing to buying anything. Everybody is out to rip you off.2. The street sellers are dishonest. If you give them a high value note they will try not give you any change. I ended up losing a 500k note because of this.3. Don't stop for the shoe shine/repair people who walk the streets and shout at you. One of them started putting glue on my shoe eventhough I kept saying no, they  then removed my shoe and started making holes in it threading some string through it. There was nothing wrong with my shoes and i kept saying no.l, but they continued anyway and demanded 450k. Total scammers, if any of them come near you just run away.4. The restaurants near the lake are usually a rip-off, so are the street vendors.
軽い気持ちで中に足を踏み入れましたが、歩き慣れるまでバイクに轢かれそうで大変です。異国の地だけあり、大変楽しかったです。
Has everything you need. Not much to do really, but for one night, it can be fun. You can walk the whole quarter, good for night, because typically the roads would be closed to larger vehicles.
Nice  area, shame the Vietnamese are rude and overcharge you at every chance they get. There ways of doing business ruin the country
For people from South Asia where such roads and quarters are very common, I found the same to be uninspiring and completely overrated . A waste of time

I come from a 3rd world country myself, so the place has little to add to me. Narrow streets, crazy traffic -  actually the worst I have seen so far. It is a real deal trying not to get killed while crossing the street. The sidewalk is also narrow and there are motorcycles parked on it, and lots of people dealing food/souvenirs, so you will have to walk on the streets and watch for the traffic most of the time.
A good place to buy some cheap snacks or shampoo, but it's hectic. The shops are also very repetitive, so you don't need to spend long here
Old town of Hanoi is chaotic, dirty and smoggy. The most interesting part was watching people as they went about their business in a frantic environment

Old quarter is crowded with narrow lanes  and unpleasant because of food prepared and served in the street.we stayed in Art trendy hotel and their customer service is super,other than that we did not bother to spend much time in Hanoi.We waliked  around the lake and that was just ok.

Basically it's Hanoi. Nothing fantastic about it. Tons of honking (and mostly it's for no good reason), rude people (younger generation Vietnamese are very courteous and don't give me crap like 'what the older adults went through').Lots of shopping (dude, so long as u don't speak their language, your price is easily doubled and stop comparing with EUR or USD and it ain't cheap!), drinking, food and yeah, avoiding traffic.Most of the sites are within walking distance so it basically displays what Hanoi has. I'd suggest, just 2 days and that's it. Else, get choked by poor air quality and noise pollution.
I was there for about 7 days, which is way too long if you consider how many more beautiful places Vietnam has to offer.

First, don't be fooled into taking a taxi off the streets. Get a hotel to call a cab or better yet use an app. Believe me you'd be saving way more than your money. Next, do not by any means show your indecisive nature here while shopping. Merchants and anybody selling something will abuse it and stalk you even right outside your hotel till you make the purchase. Nobody will be concerned about your personal space. The rush of surviving each time crossing the road would be a highlight for this area. Cliche shopping area with nothing special. You will encounter two extremes when it comes to clothes. First is the high end designer clothing ranging from 900,000 dong to worse quality known to man for 50,000 dong which is both not worth it in comparison to Bangkok Chatuchak. Very little law enforcement around so please watch your children and valuables.
治安が悪くて危ないのではなく、交通ルールが完全に無視されていて危ないという意味です。バイクの排煙はすごいし、信号に関係なく走ってるし、最高４人乗りを見ました。
ガイドさんの説明は軽く受けて、ファミリーで旧市街へ。3人だったけど、みんなで渡れば怖くない…(;^_^A  ほ、本当に…   バイク！バイク！ どうしてこれで、旅行中に大きな事故を見なかったか不思議。あと、マスクは持参しましょう。排気ガスすごいです。現地の人はマイマスクあります。埃すごいです。
With the price of living there .....It is not worth a visit. The traffic is far too dangerous. The city is dirty and disorganise. Everything is was too overpriced. Wont visit again.
ベトナムのイメージのままを感じられる、まさに異国情緒あふれる場所です。街歩きが好きでそれは楽しいのですが、バイクのクラクション音が気になりすぎて、とても疲れてしまいました。
ハノイの街を歩きながら散策すると必ずと言っていいほど旧市街を通るでしょう。オペラハウスの前には現地の人が座って休憩などしているので観光地ではないきがする
It's simply not what it used to be....busy bussle so keep this part of your trip short and head out of Hanoi.

We jumped on a bicycle cab and got a tour around the old quarter mid afternoon.  It was slow, noisy and very crowded and we could have easily walked faster and seen more.  There are heaps of shops especially those offering Northface adventure gear that seems authentic at very cheap prices, like AU$30 for a gortex jacket.  Lots of street food outlets etc.  So go ahead and do a lap on foot.  It's actually better at night when they close the area to vehicles and you can stroll more leisurely!
Il faut savoir sortir des rues touristiques et s'enfoncer un peu. Lac sympathique, personnes agréables. Attention en traverdant ! Et mefiez vous des arnaques au taxi. Ville fatiguante à la longue.
買東西或是吃喝，注意店家的表情臉很臭的盡量避開，這種人都比較會坑外國人。最好事先確定價錢再吃喝，老闆是老女人的話就不要去買，除非有標價。
When ever you are out you are constantly crossing the roads which, to be honest, ruined my stay in Hanoi,  THE TRAFFIC IS MENTAL! Constantly on edge!
Want to dodge impatient motor bike riders even when the streets are closed off to traffic, go to the old quarter. Many shops to choose from. Very hot at this time of the year. W
No disfrutamos demasiado del Old Quarter: ruidoso, estresante, maloliente, sucio, sin ningún encanto. Lo que más disfrutamos fueron de un par de restaurantes estupendos, el New day y el Orchid.

Кошмар - вот мое первое впечатление от Старого квартала Ханоя. Пешеходные дорожки забиты мотоциклами, столиками с едой. Люди сидят на тротуаре и готовят еду, тут же люди едят, другие моют посуду. Пройти и нормально рассмотреть интересные дома просто невозможно, потому что приходится идти на проезжей части,по которой беспорядочно едут мотоциклы, велосипеды и машины. В общем это место не для слабонервных, а для любителей экстремальных походов.
Too busy for comfort. Just too much street life to take in. Traffic jammed up everywhere. No safe place to walk.
We spent 2 days at this city and found the traffic was chaotic in Hanoi :(Don't really enjoy the messy Old Quarter with lots of motorcyclists keep horning on the road :(

The old quarter is packed with a huge range of shops that sell a large variety of products. A traveler not familiar to the area should keep his or her eyes open for scams or pick pockets. One of the kind shop owners reminded us to watch out for our back packs as there might be thieves who can easily make off with the valuables inside. You may not be aware of the stealing at all!
very poor air quality due to heavy traffic.NEVER BOOK ANYTHING WITH HAPRO TRAVEL SERVICE.sold train ticket cost vnd73,000 at 200,000.rip-off !!

I love Hanoi but I dislike the Old Quarter. It's a shame when I meet travellers who've been to Hanoi and talk about how congested and commercial it is. That's only the Old Quarter - the rest of the city is friendly and laid-back.Old Quarter traffic is horrible. I know Hanoi's traffic is dangerously insane everywhere, but the Old Quarter is impossible.The streets are dominated by binge-drinking tourists at night and predatory traders during the day. There is some decent food, but a lot of it is the same as anywhere else for three times the price.The advantages are the large number of cheap hostels and hotels, and plenty of opportunities to meet other foreigners. I understand why people stay in the Old Quarter, but I would recommend seeing some of the other parts of the city.

I've made mention (probably too many times now) throughout my reviews about how much I love Vietnam and how my experiences here 12 years ago, backpacking my way through SE Asia were not only formative but have, I hope on occasion made me a better person, or at least try to be. For a reasonably introverted british 20 something the encounters that I had here truly informed me how you could trust some strangers as a guest to their land (while of course, not being a complete naive) and even more importantly what, to me at least, the honest measure of success and happiness actually could be to a person. To be a kind and honest person, loved by others and be able to, on a good day, selfless for the sake of another, without the expectation of reward or prize. A little hippyish perhaps but something that had very much stuck with me and during the 2 years or so that my partner and I have been working slavishly to attempt s re-visit to this part of the world I cannot tell you how excited I was to share with her some of the incredible places that I had seen over a decade ago. Now here is the odd bit.... as we planned our journey I said on several occasions something like this..."I loved most of the places I visited in Vietnam but personally, I felt more welcomed and was treated more hospitably in Hanoi, and although I did like Saigon, there was perhaps a little too much of the giant metropolis about it for me the last time, although I am excited to return, it is Hanoi and the Old Quarter that I cannot wait to see again..." We also heard many similar opinions from others that had made the journey more recently. So my the first surprise was that when we touched the ground in HCMC and that this time we were both immediately swept away by the manic energy of the eclectic city, it is manic, it is alive, and it has certainly not been made slave to the tourist dollar and while the place is happy to humour you it is it's own beast and while there you are just another tiny cell rushing about its immense body. I really didn't expect to be so enamoured with its incredible spirit on this visit but what can I say, I missed it as soon as I left. But that didn't stop me almost wanting to run like a boy towards Hanoi, a city that took me in and fed me some of the most delicious food I have ever tasted, made me laugh side by side with people I would never see again, despite barriers of language and culture, and made me absolutely feel like a honoured guest travelling their way through this fine country. The second surprise came when I realised that was not the city nor was the Old Quarter the are that I have found waiting for me this time. I am no longer a guest, I am merely a tourist. Now not everywhere here, that would not be fair of me to say. I have not been overcharged, fleeced and generally made to feel like the great fat western pigeon that I surely look like by everyone I have encountered but by enough that for me, the magic of this city is no longer here. It no doubt left around the time that the first KFC opened up next to the pretty Hoan Kiem Lake I imagine. To be a visitor that has to be so very aware of touts and extortionists is no great leap for a person that grew up on the streets of London and of course, everyone is allowed to make their own dollar in their own way but that was not how I was made to feel when I was wandering these very Old Quarter alleyways over a decade ago and it is that sense of being a guest that I miss now. At that time I would hardly let a vendor pass me without wanting to sample their mouthwatering wares and on more than one occasion was politely told that I offered too much for such a simple delicacy and discreetly had my funds deposited helpfully but firmly back into my overly willing hands. That simply wont be the case now, and I feel that my partner and I, despite having worked like dogs to make this opportunity be made a reality for us now, are walking dollar sign targets waiting to be exploited by the next donut waving vendor. A greater shame, as we now feel our hearts sink at anything without a price next to it and the phrase Qua nhieu (too much) the only thing that I want emblazoned on a cap or t-shirt cynically waved in my face when trying to cross the road. Compared to the joyous anonymity that you can still receive when Saigon graciously allows you to walk its streets and the twinkle still present in the eyes of the market stall vendors there when they know that you are quite onto their amusing pitch that "this is the very last one!" then give me the manic, maddening bustle of HCMC any day now. And for me ultimately that is surprise number 3! I guess these days I'm just a Saigon man after all.
日曜夜。夜店も沢山でて楽しいそぞろ歩きのはずでしたが、ここを出てタクシーに乗ったときに鞄を切られて財布をすられていることに気がつきました。警察で事情聴衆を受けたところ、カッターで10cmほどザクッと切り込み財布のみを持って行くのがここらの流儀らしい。いやあ、生まれて初めての体験。実に巧妙なテクニックで持って行かれるので要注意です。
Le "old quarter" est surtout composé d'échoppes de vêtements, chaussures, et cochonneries en tous genres. Aucune authenticité.

Whatever charm there was in this area has been lost to overcrowding, souvenir shops, hawkers and crazy moped traffic. We stayed at the Essence Hotel due to the good reviews (and the hotel itself was very good), but in hindsight I would have stayed in the French Quarter or other parts of town where it was a bit less chaotic.

The Old Quarter has narrow roads, lots of people and motor bikes and cars and heavy pollution. I can think of very little to recommend it. Many of the shop(?)keepers attempted (and succeeded) to rip us off on numerous occasions. Unscrupulous.
La estampa del Old Quarter es bonita e interesante, pero no hay aceras para caminar, es inseguro para el peatón. Hay mucha contaminación de aire y de ruido.

To visitors, it's best if you don't let the people who sell things to touch you, this range from; 'the shoe cleaner', 'fruit seller' and 'donut seller'. These people gives you the service first, even with you not wanting/require them, and charges you for 'service rendered'. Do not trust them even when they said 'No money'. Basically, these scams spoils what to be a very nice holiday. These scammers should know that by 'earning' money through scamming is not cool, they potentially not helping in attracting tourist into their country.

The Golden Lotus Luxury Motel was very comfortable  and lovely friendly staff willing to do anything to make our stay comfortable. The area is a little dirty but typical of old  cities where they have been built with narrow streets and have to deal with lots of traffic and people.
Чего-то необычного не смог заметить. Только огромное количество жующих и едящих местных жителей. Да и повсюду местные магазины.

I have been tremendously disappointed with my overall Hanoi visit.  After almost one month travelling around Indochina (mainly Thailand and Cambodia) and thinking it could not get worst, I experienced Hanoi as the biggest lie of my whole trip.  Aggresive town, highly poluted, noisy, dirty, horrible traffic and non-stop efforts by local tourism related workers (and other) to try to get money from you constantly, mercyless and very rudely sometimes.  Not to mention that the so-called landmark attractions are not such, too small, poorly conserved or ridiculosly valueless, and that the best Vietnamese food i ever tried was in Melbourne.The best thing of Hanoi (the only I would openly say dont miss it if you find yourself in Hanoi by accident) is the water puppets.  Very nice, traditional and charming.The other remarkable attraction in the area is Halong Bay but it is too far so make sure you consider a 4 star overnight cruise or stay in the area but book your cruise visit first and with a reliable tour operator to avoid issues, overpay, crowds or more unexpected events.

The food did not agree with me or my partner at all. I got talking to a westerner who has lived in Hanoi for 6 years now and he told me gets sick once a month periodically and so do his colleagues some of which are vietnamese locals. The hustle and bustle becomes too much after a couple days and frustration begins to build. Not a happy chappy

El casco histórico de Hanoi es, sin duda, el punto central para desplazarse por la ciudad. La mayoría de los comercios, buena parte de las atracciones y casi todos los restaurantes típicos y demás se encuentran por aquí. Sin embargo, moverse por estas calles es, salvo para el urbanita más extremo, demasiado estresante -vigilando en todo momento coches, bicis y, sobre todo, motos que no respetan ni aceras -que de hecho se emplean como parking improvisado- ni peatones ni unos a otros. Entre el ruido de los claxons y el humo de los tubos de escape, se acaba con un dolor de cabeza considerable, aparte de con la sensación de haber estado chupando un bidón de gasolina. Evidentemente, es impensable ir a Hanoi y no pasar por aquí, pero quitando la curiosidad, yo me decantaría por otros destinos.Compras imprescindibles: café local (no necesariamente el de comadreja) y pimienta

Mostly low-end retail properties - little of cultural or architectural interest. One or two interesting cafes in front of the cathedral (which was closed to visitors). Traffic manic and footpaths impossible to use because either full of parked motorcycles or pavement 'cafes'. Very disappointed!
原付バイクや自転車が行き交い、活気があるが、交通ルールを守らないことや観光名所が少ないのが残念だ。ベトナムらしさが失われ、ごちゃごちゃしすぎている感がある。

Muy caótico, sin aceras, sucio, no merece la pena. Casi todas las tiendas tienen lo mismo. Es insoportable el ruido de las motos y coches pitando en todo momento. Un ratito hace gracia, pero todo el día así termina por desesperar.

Очень не понравилось. Улочки узенькие, домики маленькие, непропорциональные, напомнили мне скворечники))) Очень грязно и шумно. В принципе, страны юго-восточной Азии не отличаются чистотой, но Ханой мне показался особенно грязным: люди все делают в одном месте: едят прямо на улице, у дороги, в пыли, тут же выкидывают мусор и тут же чем-то приторговывают. Вообще Ханой мне показался одним сплошным рынком... Толпа....Толкотня... Смешение разных запахов, преимущественно неприятных... Азиатский колорит, можно сказать, но  меня не привлекает...Движение - вообще сумасшедшее.... Чтобы перейти дорогу поначалу требуется изрядная сноровка и даже смелость, потом правда привыкаешь и перестаешь считать это подвигом) Местные достопримечательности  особого восторга не вызвали. Скудно, не поражает. Единственное, что понравилось: местная еда и искреннее, доброжелательное отношение к людям. И еще запомнился смешной номер в гостинице:  двухэтажный, с террасой. Кстати гостиница очень неплохая ( Hanoi moment hotel 2), отличный сервис, вкусные и обильные завтраки.Так что если и заезжать в Ханой - то только транзитом, в качестве отправной точки для перемещения по другим местам, не более того. Я вот поехала в двухдневный круиз по бухте Халонг - всего 4 часа пути от Ханоя - и вы окажетесь в удивительном и очень необычном месте!!

You have to look petty hard to see beyond the mounds of Chinese junk to are what this place might have looked like, a long time ago. I unfortunately failed to see the overall beauty and interest others rage about. A half an hour wander is plenty of time spent here.

Old quarter is a must experience. People are very friendly. Traffic is mad-looking but you soon realise that it's very tolerant and fluent. I haven't seen 1 accident. Overall, given it's all dirty, smelly, noisy, ugly, I would recommend to also experience some more beautiful elements of the Vietnamese reality.
really is not good for walking, vehicles here too crowded, narrow roads, the tent to visit the old town.

I did not like the Old Quarter at all.  The streets are narrow and completely taken over by motor bikes and scooters.  It's noisy and very dirty with little in the way of interesting buildings in mitigation.  On the plus side, there are some interesting bars and restaurants.

I like the concept of cyclos for short distances in Hanoi, for both environmental and aesthetic reasons.  But I had two ventures on Cyclos in Hanoi last week (Nov 30, 2014), both ending is disputes over the cost...so my guess is this is a common problem.In both cases, I agreed with the cyclo drivers prior on a price, having both told them the destinations and then pin-pointing them on a street map. The first ride went fine until the end, when payment was due.  Then the amount jumped from 300,000 Dong to 450,000. It wasn't a big deal, but it wasn't what we had agreed upon. I paid it.For the second ride, from north end of Hoan Kiem Lake to the Hoa Lu prison, I told the driver the destination, and showed him on the map.  He nodded and then responded."museum".  I said, "No...Prison", pointing again at the map. He nodded, and off we went. When I realized he missed an intersection, I again showed him the map and repeated "Hoa Lu Prison" He nodded and repeated "Museum".  When we ended up at the Women's Museum, I again pointed out the correct destination, and this time he got it and took me there. However, when we got there, the price jumped from 30,000 D to 100,000. We debated the amount for over a minute, at which time I gave him 50,000 D and told him that was the most I would pay. He scowled at me and rode off. I sincerely hate the appearance of being an ugly American, but in these two cases, it was clear to me that at least some cyclo drivers are trying to scam tourists. There's no way cyclos should cost more that taxis!
Der er en del scoter på gaderne man skal virkelig passe på hvor man går sælgerne er meget sultne så blev ved med at gå og lad være med at røre på noget. Ellers en halv dags tur  er fint.

As a tour leader from groups I use to stay every time in the old quarter.Its just to overcrowded and what annoys me the most is that you can not walk on the sidewalks , these are motorbike parking's and/or shop extensions . This forces you to walk on the streets where cars and especially motorbike has no respect whatsoever for pedestrians. the Vietnamese government school really do something about this problems that's scares tourists away from the quarter

Typical Chinese market area with every square inch dedicated to shops.  Lots of pesky hawkers trying to extract every tourist $$. Difficult to navigate and enjoy the architecture with lots of scooters buzzing around. Highly over-rated.
Life just goes on, no order loud noisy and messy - That's my view maybe I will adjust myself and handle it differently.

Too many people trying to sell me things along the way. Beware of this basket carrying lady who would dump the baskets on you and request for a photo. And then... yes you've guessed it right, she will demand money from you.

Feels like a bad movie that you cannot escape from! Have never been treated so badly as we were there by rude, unfriendly people who constantly tried ripping us off... Hot and humid and smelly, unbelievable traffic, dogs on the menu.... I wish we escaped to Hoi An or Sapa earlier.... Good to experience it but 1 day would have been more than enough for us...
l'état de délabrement de la vieille ville , trottoirs défoncés, façades des maisons pas netretenues depuis plus de 50 ans, sans doute tout cela dû à une politique déficiente des autorités municipales

Personally think Hanoi is over-rated as a tourist destination. Firstly things are almost always way more expensive than the South. Secondly, while both HCMC and Hanoi have traffic issues, HCMC has made a few attempts to provide pedestrain crossing points-not so in Hanoi- not even in heavy tourist areas. After spending an hour or two walking the Old Quarter, my eyes and throat started to burn from the air pollution.Apart from a few nice eateries which you can find else-where, the Old quarter is mostly filled with the same kind of shops you find else where in Vietnam and the night-markets are yet another Chinese fleamarket found all over the globe.
se voce conhece São Paulo e já foi na 25 de março, o Old Quarter de Hanoi é uma grande 25 de março. Muitas lojinhas com um monte de coisa feia e qualidade duvidosa.

The old quarter of Hanoi is very busy with people and traffic, it's impossible to walk round and stay on the pavement as the pavement is full of parked motorbikes and people cooking on the street, the streets are unsanitary with cooking waste just tipped in the gutter and you have to share the road with the hectic undisciplined traffic, each street seems to have a theme, shoe street, toy street etc. and the goods for sale are very similar, many top brand name fakes here.It could be said that the area has character but I wouldn't consider trying to walk this area with a young child.This is my personal view, some people may like the area but not for me.

We are  quite experienced travellers but on our first night in Hanoi went to the old quarter thinking we could get around ourselves. First mistake was to trust a cyclo. Do not do this under any circumstance! Not only did he want to charge us Twice after an agreed price but four times. He started an argument on the street that we were not going to win so ...... Just don't do itWe ate but watched a big cockroach wander over everything until it landed on my shoulder. If you are going to eat at the beer street intersection try  Cafe 69. Much better establishment
Visita cumplidora, no hay q esperar mucho.Vale la pena recorrer la zona caminando, y la mejor experiencia es cruzar una calle ancha

The old quarter in hanoi is almost disappear, the veitnamese don' t protect the ols history and they are building a new towers where before was the old quarter.The old quarter is noises, very dungerous about the trafic, the road is not close and the people are like krazy driving the moto.Finally theris notting to see, notting to buy, just one hour around and notting more.
The traffic is worse & everyone ride crazily. Please pay more attention on your safety and valuable things.

The hot and humid weather makes wandering difficult enough without being constantly forced off the pavements, and the shade provided, by parked motorbikes and having to walk in the street dodging motorbikes coming from every direction.  Also many photo opportunities are spoiled by thick dangling strands of electrical cables and ugly transformers.  Much very loud hooting by taxis, etc.  This might have been a pleasant experience once upon a time, but not now.   Maybe a few exist, but we didn't see any pedestrian-only streets - the city authorities should make a network of shopping streets pedestrian only and enforce it rigorously.  They do it elsewhere in the world why not here?

Too crowded with no room to walk on sidewalks or streets for that matter.  Although loads of good street food the sidewalks need to be cleared for pedestrians use.  Cars, trams, motos, cyclos and taxis all honking because people have to walk on the streets.  Old Quarter is not for the faint of heart or anyone who has trouble manuevering around while walking.

Old Quarter is crowded with people spewing out on to the pavements so you have to walk in the gutter or road, it's very dirty, the people don't seem very friendly and numerous people are trying to scam you the whole time. I would recommend the French Quarter a much nicer area. Also be prepared to see shocking sights like loads of puppies crammed in cages for sale and roasted dog to buy to eat!!

Ну, я тоже там побывала, и что? Сказать, что это какая-то особенная экзотика я не могу. Сказать, чтобы очень понравилось  - тоже. Ну так, район, где торгуют всяким хламом, сувенирами и т.д. Подобные места я уже видела и они мне никогда не нравились, какие-то они стремные, что-ли. Вот район рядом с площадью Бадинь мне понравился намного больше. Там все аккуратно, никакого мусора, красивые здания во французском стиле, цветущие деревья, здания посольств - все эстетично. Здесь же - в старом квартале - шум, суета и грязь. Единственное преимущество - близость к достопримечательностям ну и, пожалуй, недорогие отели.

I guess we expected something different. A visit to the Old Quarter is an experience of it's own. It's crowded, dirty, worn down and fast moving. We expected better food nicer people. Maybe we expected too much after our Bangkok experience.

Улицы делятся по специализациям, что удобно при поиске, например, какого-нибудь определенного товара, но если попал на улицу, которая тебе не нужна, то придется топать мимо нескончаемых рядов одних и тех же тазиков или запчастей к велосипедам. Грязно, шумно, улицы очень узкие, тротуары завалены товаром и табуретками, очень много народа, особенно туристов, и все это вперемешку с несущимися мотоциклами.
Personally I could not care less for the smell, noise and dirt all over the Old part of Hanoi, but truly understand this is what it is in Hanoi and there is something special about it.

Sorry but dont like it here and will not be back. Have been here a week, it is absolute madness, there are 4 million motorbikes here and I am sure they just point the bike and hope for the best.I hate that they eat cats and dogs, they are hung up and smoked its terrible. We did a food on foot  tour which was very good and informative. We did the perfume pagoda tour today, 2 hours to get to it, 45 mins in a sanpan (they have thousands of them) then one almighty walk up hundreds of steps, also a chair lift ride. Was quite interesting though. We did other tours too. But its not for me, off to Hoi Ann tomorrow so I have been told is very nice there.

Yes, some find old buildings to look at and, yes, plenty of walking to be had, but add in the very real dangers in the form of the non-stop traffic and horrendous air pollution, the relentless noise, the filth and the trash, I would have to rate the Old Quarter as one of the less desirable destinations in a city that is at best a 'challenge' for the visitor.
道路をうめつくすバイクバイクバイク。バイクに乗ったまま買い物をする人たちが歩行者の通路を防ぎ、バイクのクラクションと排気ガスで、細い路地が埋め尽くされていました。玩具のまち、珈琲のまち、お正月用品のまち・・・あぁつかれた。もういい、って感じです。

был вечером на байке. искал рынок. капец грязно, шумно. улицы тесные. но не столь) порадовал супчик бунчао) зашелв шаурмешную) не дорого и очень вкучно) НО,когда заезжал на байке утонул в куче)было не очень приятно)

Шумно, грязно - то, что не доели, выливают прям на дорогу, и рыбные кости и лапша, очень неприятно. Что из себя представляет Старый квартал - барахолка, море народа, все куда-то идут, бегут и тп. Тут же на табуретках кушают, тут же обувь примеряют, это не мое.
The area looks more like a market for cheap and low-quality goods.Район больше похож на рынок дешевых и некачественных товаров.

This area is so crowded, noisy and needs a good cleaning. The traffic is so congested and trying to cross a street is a feat in itself! The sidewalks are full of parked motorbikes and locals cooking and eating that visitors have to walk in the narrow streets. Everything is so jammed in you cannot even see the ancient temples. It is too bad!
色々な通りがあり散策と冷やかしには面白い場所ですが、通りに入ると現在地が解らなく場合がありますので気を付けて散策を楽しんで下さい。
It all looked the same to me. I couldn't find any wow factor. Disliked the touts following me for block after block - worse than in most other Asian places. Oh well …

This part of Hanoi is mad, so noisy, a complete nut house. The motorbikes are everywhere so to cross a road is a real challenge despite we have managed it all the time without any problems...just start to cross and the drivers go around you. The constant horn blowing makes it very noisy and after a while you are just so sick of the noise you really can't be bothered to look at any shops or anything else. Everything we saw in shops looked like junk to us anyway. Lots of local women carry heavy baskets and try to put them on your shoulders which we didn't like at all. There are very few interesting sights in Hanoi and unfortunately they are all in this mad quarter so if you come to Hanoi you can't possibly avoid this place. We were very happy to leave after 2 days here.

Glad I went wouldn't go back same old same old the clyclos will try and charge you 200,000 dong an hour the real price is 100,000 and 10 mins is enough.Have a cool beer at the pub and watch the world go by.

Un entrelazado de calles estrechas en las que todos los bajos de sus típicas estrechas casas son comercios, repartido por gremios. Extranjeros y más extranjeros, con tiendas vendiendo lo mismo una y otra vez. Me dijeron que era el vietnam auténtico y la verdad es que es demasiado hecho al turista. Lo que MENOS ME HA GUSTADO DE VIETNAM. Pasaba alguna señora con las cestas a cuestas que te las cuelgan para la foto y sacar un dinerillo. Huele a Durian, que puede estar bien para el que le guste. Sin magia...No hay que perder demasiado tiempo allí.

Number one thing in Hanoi? Yeah right! This place was my least favourite part of Hanoi. It is filthy and you're walking through puddles of mysterious black liquids, people are burning trash in the roads, motorbikes are flying past about one inch from you in every direction. We tried to find a whole bunch of sites in this district that Lonely Planet recommended and all of them either didn't exist or were so unremarkable that they were indistinguishable from everything else.

We were told how charming the Old Quarter of Hanoi was. To be honest, we found it to be very dirty with very few points of genuine interest and devoid of any sort of charm. Regretted not staying in our hotel and enjoying the facilities.

I felt the need to write this review because I feel like I went into Hanoi blind. The reviews I read on both trip advisor and elsewhere led me to believe that the old quarter would be a more manageable, slightly upscale area of Hanoi. I did not find this to be the case at all. Though it is definitely worth seeing if you are going to Hanoi, I expected something along the lines of Greenwich village in New York City and found it to be much more comparable to downtown Detroit. Though every so often you came across a cute cafe or boutique style store, by and large expect to find store upon store of imported cheap goods (ie a store filled with cheap shoes or floor fans or stuffed animals) with only a brief sighting of anything worth stopping for every few blocks. Though a nice place to acquire some good street food, even that is hard to navigate as there are so many options its hard to know what is actually good/relatively clean. In addition, almost all of the streets have had their sidewalks turned into parking spots for motorbikes, leaving no room for walking and forcing you out into the streets. An uncomfortable position to be in when walking through a city infamous for drive-by motorbike purse snatchings. Though slightly more manageable in the evening, the hustle and bustle during the day in this part of town plus the heat of the summer made this a stressful area to navigate, as opposed to a nice part of town through which to wander. The one thing that helped alter my opinion of this part of town was the evening food tour we did through Vietnam Awesome Travel with a fantastic guide who helped us find great local joints/ street food. All in all: if you are going to Hanoi, it is important to see this part of town, and Hoan Kiem Lake is a nice relief from the craziness of the Old Quarter but by and large, go in expecting this to be exhausting and overwhelming. (Note: I live in this region, I LOVED Ho Chi Minh City, and I am accustomed to big cities). Side note- I can't imagine trying to navigate this area with young children. It would truly be a complete nightmare.

I won't argue with what everyone else wrote about Hanoi, I can see where they are coming from, but I did not enjoy the Old Quarter nearly as much as most people seem to. My experience was probably highly influenced by the fact that it rained both days I was there. The piles of garbage on the sidewalk (most of which is not in bags) tends to spew all over the place when the heavy rain starts. Quite unpleasant to walk through. Also, while the traffic is no crazier than anywhere else in S.E. Asia, sidewalks are reserved for motorbike parking, so you need to get used to walking with the motorbike and car traffic on the road. For the most part this is not a bit deal, but if that kind of thing makes you nervous (like it did my gf) you will not enjoy your time here. If you can deal with these things, and the constant honking, you will probably enjoy the life of the city quite a bit.

After plenty of researching telling me the Old Quarter is where any tourist HAS to be, we spent 3 nights there. It was really hectic and dirty. We even cut our trip short from four nights to three in favour of some extra days in central vietnam. There are not many bins around, so rubbish is thrown in the street to decompose there instead. There are some street cleaners, but not enough for the amount of rubbish generated. We brought back the mentality that this is a pretty close experience to what Vietnam would have been like in earlier times. Two nights would be ample here.The street food that we tried (came recommended by different blogs) was bun cha, which was rather bland, and crab spring rolls, which were amazing! Best part of the Old Quarter experience. Oh, and hair cuts are cheap - even at the salons.

A part Hoi An, les grandes villes vietnamiennes (Da Nang, Saïgon, Hanoï) n'ont pas grand chose à offrir... La "vieille ville" de Hanoï est difficile à trouver dans le ce bazar de circulation, vieux immeubles sales, magasins criards et fils électriques entremêlés. On est assurément loin des hutong pékinois et vieilles demeures japonaises, et il est difficile de trouver charme ou intérêt à quelques détails d'architecture coloniale ou plus authentiquement vietnamien complètement enfouis. Restent finalement quelques bâtiments coloniaux d'architecture française, et c'est bien dommage vu le passé houleux qu'on en soit réduit à visiter cela (la moitié étant occupé par le PC local ou des administrations).Donc on passera là de manière obligée pour aller à la belle baie d'Halong (4 insupportables heures sur les routes d'une lenteur exaspérante), sans étendre le séjour, et on se promènera au sud-est du lac Ho-Kiem pour les bâtiments coloniaux et au nord pour les quelques rues un peu typiques près du marché de nuit

Trying to saunter along the overcrowded pavements in holiday mode with the masses of mopeds flying about the narrow roads and on the pavements and parked indiscriminately on the pavements, with horns constantly blaring as they narrowly miss each other a nerve jangling , teeth grinding exercise in tolerance. Throw in 40 degrees heat and frequent harassment by the locals trying to,sell sunglasses, guide books and deep fried thingies... I think I prefer the quieter side of this beautiful country
Its so beatiful but its very dirty the local people att the on motobike with the horn so cazy. For me very hard for me to pass the street there!becarful

Beware and set the price first. Our hotel receptionist told us it was 80,000 dong ($4) for an hour, discussed this with the cyclo driver and it was agreed. 35 minutes into the ride our cyclo driver told us time was up. We insisted it wasn't. He then asked for more money to return us half an hour away from our hotel. We insisted he take us back to the hotel where he wanted to dump us off several doors down (I would think so hotel staff couldn't see him). He then got really angry and said the cost was 200,000 dong (remember we were quoted 80,000). I don't mind tipping and paying extra, but not when I am blatantly being ripped off. We gave him 100,000 in the end as he wouldn't budge from 200,000 and walked off.. The receptionist came outside and had an argument with him. Made what should have been a fun thing to do very disappointing. Before you get onto the rickshaw show the correct money to the driver and insist on the time too to hopefully prevent this thing happening to you.

you will see the same products 100 times over, and nothing of any quality. Filthy, dirty location with people constantly hassling you and trying to rip you off with overvalued charges.  If you miss seeing the old quarter / you've not missed anything!!

It's great fun, and the shopping and eating are good, but ultimately Hanoi is a disappointment.  It's not nearly as attractive as the guide books make out, its streets are dirty, narrow and dark, and there is almost no green space.  After 2 weeks travelling around Vietnam I felt let down by Hanoi.  So the markets are busy and bustling and the traffic is chaos, but that is true of every town in Vietnam. Saigon is a much more attractive city.

Worst part of Hanoi visit.  It is not charming.  It is dirty and anything you would buy here to take with you as a traveler is cheaply made.  They would do well to cut car/scooter traffic and get the crap out of there.  Hoi An is a good model for what this could be.

We were rather underwhelmed by the old quarter; we actually walked through without realising that was it!Perhaps because it was February and poor weather we didn't see the quarter at its best: its certainly not the most attractive place we've seen by a wide margin.Going during the tet festival at least made the place alive with millions of people celebrating in a very small space; great fun

I bought a few great 'copies' of clothing.  A New Face jacket and packpack, a purse and typical souveniers. It was hit or miss with restaurants, i had some pretty disgusting meals in really shabby places.  I found a few restaurants with great vietnamese food but they were quite expensive. The traffic and lack or road rules was overwhelmingly busy and I found it hard to walk through the Old Quater and cross busier intersections.Many of the attractions outside of the Old Quater are a few hours away (at least).  It made the Old Quater stay a poor choice on with my planning.I had a hard time avoiding men trying to stick glue into my sandals (i don't know why they wre trying too).  I was constantly followed by taxi's asking why i wouldn't get in the car.   The abundance of rats a complete turn off, I didn't like the Old Quater at all.

Some revel in it - I found it a pain. So noisy and dirty - what nice things there are cannot be admired because you have to keep moving - you'll be in the way of traffic or street vendors or hawkers will want to sell you something. It's exhausting. It may have been good once but it's simply too chotic and crowded to enjoy but good to experience. Makes all other cities seem serene. Crossing the road ok once you're used to it but driving here is insane.

The Old Quarter is a mess. No attempt has been made to make this fascinating neighborhood safe to walk. The narrow streets are clogged with every conceivable method of transport, so the wide sidewalks are used to park motorbikes and set up food stalls. You can only walk in the street, and it's scary and tiresome if you have chosen to stay here. Visiting, it might be kinda fun dodging motos and taxis, but not all day, all night.

Hanoi não é uma cidade para caminhar, o trânsito é caótico, estacionam na calçada e a motos também circulam nas caçaldas. Por este motivo andar pelo Old Quarter, que não tem nada de interessante, além de estar mal indicado e mal conservado, não vale a pena

Interesting to walk around and buy typical souvenirs but not specially of good quality.  Really difficult to walk on the sidewalks as are occupied by parked motorbikes or stalls.  Noisy, what you think Vietnam is...

The lake in the old quarter is dead , the garbage and upkeep of most hotels is poor , the streets are un safe for walkers. crossing the street safely is difficult , and can be dangerous, the sidewalks are blocked with motor bikes and little food stands , so you have to walk in the dangerous street.

We had just arrived at Hanoi international airport after 9 pm. We checked into our hotel in the old quarter and went looking for some street food, and to soak up the ambience of old Hanoi.It was well after 10 pm. We set out from our hotel looking to eat somewhere locally.  Nearby on the corner of Ma May and Luong Ngoc Quyen Sts we found an outdoor street BBQ place. We were not shown a menu, but also we did not ask for prices (more fool us). We were cordially shown to a low table with those low stools. Bia LaRue was forthcoming, as was the portable BBQ. Then 2 plates of raw beef and raw vegetables were produced. The staff were very attentive and helpful.The staff poured some oil on the hotplate and showed us how to BBQ the beef and the vegetables. On request more Bia LaRue was supplied.It was good for street food and a quite pleasant experience, with lots of locals also eating there.Then came the Hanoi haircut. They brought the bill. Each plate of raw beef and vegetables, enough to feed two people, was 400,000 dong, or US $20 a plate. All up the bill, including beer was just shy of 1,000,000 dong, or US $50.We had been ripped off or in the local vernacular been given a haircut in a big way. We tried to negotiate the bill down, we called our hotel to get them to talk to the vendors, but the vendors would not be moved.We finally paid 1,000,000 dong for good but what was after all street food. The ringleader of the haircut gang walked away with one of the biggest smiles on his face that I have seen.Some people in Vietnam think that tourists are fair game and wont be back again. So they think it is OK to riff them off for as much as they can get away with.My advice is do not eat on On the corner of Ma May and Luong Ngoc Quyen Streets.

Interesting to visit, with shops still clustered together by type on certain roads, however traffic is a real problem. All Vietnamese cities seem very busy but the Old Quater Hanio is simply insane, very narrow to start with, shops spilling out onto the steets, any pavements = scooter parking, what is left of the road (that you have to walk down) is packed with scooters, cars, motorbikes, bikes and pedestrians. The few streets that were almost pedestrianised (well no cars) were just as bad. Something needs to be done, imagine motorway traffic volume forced onto a country lane.

Teeming hordes of horn happy scooters and cars who show no fear of death, theirs or yours.  Sidewalks are mostly impassible due to restaurants, parked scooters, etc- so pedestrians have to frequently take to the side of the road, where scooters whizz by close enough to whisper in your ear.  Crossing the street is high stakes frogger.  Maybe worth a visit in one of the bicycle rickshaws, but is not pleasant place to stroll.
Too many tourists and shops catering only for tourists. It could be in any city in the world - very disappointing after the rest of our wonderful holiday
too crowded difficult to navigate we are not backpackers looking for the "lost" experience mature travellers would be better staying outside the old quarter

Bellissime le abitazioni, ristrutturate, decadenti, terrazzate, floreali, colorate.. ce n'è per tutti i gusti. Ma per apprezzare appieno il luogo occorrerebbe camminare a naso all'insù. Perchè ad altezza uomo.. ormai si vedono solo più negozi di souvenir, agenzie viaggi che vendono escursioni, ed hotel. Dimenticatevi i negozietti locali. Qui ormai tutto è a misura di turista!
Yes, chaos everywhere -- on the streets, on the pavements (if you can find any), at the shops . . . not really a nice place to be in for a long time.

Where to start.. could not walk on the footpath at all and had to resort to taking great leaps of faith by manourvering our progress on the road. The biggest danger were the bike riders who were texting and not looking, the rest were pretty good...Never, ever let a pineapple seller try to put there harness on you for a photo opportunity...one tried with me and when i refused to accept her harness she pushed me into the traffic and another threatened me with a Parang around the Lake when i respectfully declined to purchase a pineapple "no thankyou" should not invoke that sort of hostility. Unlikely to return

The Old Quarter, seen it, done it.  Will I want to do it again, seriously doubt it.  It's where the scooters OWN the road.  You don't just look left and right, you gotta look front and centre, they will come at you at all possible angles.  Ignore pedestrian crossings, they don't mean anything here.  It didn't help that we came from spending a week at an isolated island, coming here was a shock to the system.  Unfortunately, I didn't find it all that interesting, kinda overrated.  I had hoped to find bargains, it wasn't that cheap at all.   I did find a pretty painting of the Vietnamese landscape which I love.  BEWARE of PICKPOCKETS there are plenty here, they are pro's. I did enjoy watching the people, the craziness of it all, an interesting experience no doubt.  Will I do it again, like I said, I seriously doubt it.
This was the noisiest and most chaotic place we went to in Vietnam. After being there we wish we had spent more time elsewhere.
Need to make sure you've had your coffee before you set out in the old quarter. Make sure you bargain.
I found that has not the personality of Vietnam and also found it very dirty and messy. I imagined something more sympathetic to the Old Quarter.

This was probably a true 'old quarter' some decades ago but not any more.  The sidewalks were covered with motor scooters and most of the shops sold repetitious rubbish.  There was good stuff to be found but it took a lot of work and frankly the effort wasn't worth the bother.  The only redeeming feature was the Little Hanoi Cafe on Hang Gai Street where the coffee was good.
It is not fun.  It is crowded, dirty, and dangerous.  There are not good restauarants, bars, or attractions.If that is a fun area, go ahead.  There are slums all over the world.
Never felt comfortable walking as the pavements are not useable and the motorbikes travel too fast and too close. Saw what looked like roasted pigs on display but they weren't. Very upsetting.

i really dont see what people like about this disgusting place. if risking your life crossing the street, walking on the road because the sidewalk is all taken by parking motorbikes, sidestepping rats and homeless beggars, pushing away persistant street merchants, breathing suffocating polluted air, being deafened by constant horns is your thing - the old quarter is the place for you. problem is, besides the museums, its about all this forsaken city has to offer, so you might as well take a bicycle taxi tour through it.
It would be magical if they just looked after it and cleaned it up
旅行会社のパックでシクロ体験30分。チップは1＄。シクロと言っても自転車の前に乗車台が付いているだけ。人力車のように目線が高くないしな。車道の端を走るが排気ガスが一杯。旧市街降りてから徒歩で一回り。旧市街は庶民の暮らしが見えるといいますが、わざわざ来なくてもあちらこちらで見えます。通り毎に売っているものが違います。でも品質が不安でちょっと買いづらいかも。

You have to walk in the road as stall-holders use the pavements to display their wares.  There are scooters everywhere (we were told there are 3.5 million in the city), the traffic flow is constant and it comes at you from all directions.  We were told to cross the road you simply step out and walk slowly and steadily across.  It is truly terrifying.  We had the assistance of a couple of students from the local university.  I'm not sure we'd have managed it on our own.In the old quarter are streets with shops dedicated to a particular commodity eg shoe street, shirt street, winter street (with warm clothing).  There are many street traders selling tasty looking snacks - if you are brave enough to risk them.  The markets are very cramped and stuffed full of of all manner of goods and foods.  The meat and fish areas smell disgusting.  You can take a kind of rickshaw ride if you prefer not to walk.  The rickshaws consist of a basket on the front of a bicycle.  The passenger sits in the basket and so is in the firing line as far as oncoming traffic is concerned.There are various Buddhist temples one can visit in the side streets.  The thing not to miss is probably the water puppet theatre.

The Old Qarter is noisy and insane with traffic. Most of the time the sidewalk is blocked with motor bikes, or vendors and you have to walk on the road. Wait until you just see motor bikes coming and then put out your hand and walk slowly across the streets. It's very scary!!!

Funny to see the traffic there for the first 5 minutes. Gives you headache before the end of the hour.Try their yellow ice-cold drink with a slice of lemon for 5000$ ! Good and even better taken on the side-walk 20cm above the ground. :)
Sidewalks here are for motorcycle parking, business wares, you are forced to walk in the traffic

Great food and people but leave your lungs at home ! Terrible air quality , if ypu have alergies stay away unless you want to dose yourself everyday with anti hystamines like my traveling companions had to do. Hard to walk anywhere becauae the millions of motorbikes park on the sidewalk so you must walk on the road in traffic. Very noisy from the constant honking and competing bar music. However I sure enjoyed all the smiling locals and would love to return when they can figure out how to reduce the smog.

Lots of street food to try and some interesting architecture. However, it’s an extremely busy city, making it difficult to get around without getting hit by a motorbike…even when walking on the sidewalks. If you’re looking for relaxation and moderate sanitation, Hanoi isn’t for you.
